GPT-5.5 Merged Evaluation Review Samples

Random test-set samples from Physics and FrontierPhysics. Each sample shows the problem statement, extracted boxed answer, reference final solution, and GPT-5.5 judge rationale.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

1. textbook/11.9.2

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Ornstein 6Uhlenbeck process The Ornstein 6Uhlenbeck process is the harmonic oscillator of nonequilibrium dynamics. It describes stochastic evolution in cases where the averaged dynamics of a Langevin particle is described by a linear first-order differential equation. Realizations include the dynamics of a particle subject only to friction (but no external forces), or that of an overdamped particle in a harmonic external potential. The dissipative dynamics close to extremal potential points is often described by variants of this process. (a) Consider a Brownian particle in one dimension whose velocity is governed by the stochastic differential equation v˙+γv=f(t)m,\dot{v} + \gamma v = \frac{f(t)}{m},v˙+γv=mf(t)​, (11.83) where f is a Langevin force, and ⟨f(t)f(t′)⟩=Aδ(t−t′).\langle f(t) f(t') \rangle = A \delta(t - t').⟨f(t)f(t′)⟩=Aδ(t−t′). Determine the general solution of Eq. (11.83) for the velocity with the initial condition v(0) = v_0, and calculate ⟨v(t)⟩and⟨v2(t)⟩.\langle v(t) \rangle and \langle v^2(t) \rangle.⟨v(t)⟩and⟨v2(t)⟩. Using the long-time limit of the latter, identify the coefficient A with the help of the equipartition theorem. (b) Consider the time-dependent velocity distribution p(v,t). It is governed by the Fokker 11Planck equation [∂∂t+∂∂va1(v)−12∂2∂v2a2(v)]p(v,t)=0.\left[ \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial}{\partial v} a_1(v) - \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial v^2} a_2(v) \right] p(v,t) = 0.[∂t∂​+∂v∂​a1​(v)−21​∂v2∂2​a2​(v)]p(v,t)=0. (11.84) With the help of Eq. (11.84), give equations for ∂t⟨v⟩and∂t⟨v2⟩\partial_t \langle v \rangle and \partial_t \langle v^2 \rangle∂t​⟨v⟩and∂t​⟨v2⟩ and identify the coefficients a_1 and a_2, e.g., by using the results of part (a). Give the corresponding Fokker-Planck equation for the generating function g(k,t)=∫dvg(k,t)=\int dv \\g(k,t)=∫dv; e^{-ikv} p(v,t). (c) The first-order partial differential equation (11.85) can be solved, for example with the method of characteristics. A general solution is of the form g(k,t)=e−Tk22mϕ(ke−γt)g(k,t) = e^{-\frac{T k^{2}}{2 m}} \phi \bigl(k e^{-\gamma t}\bigr)g(k,t)=e−2mTk2​ϕ(ke−γt) Determine the function ϕfromtheinitialconditionp(v,t=0)=δ(v−v0)\phi from the initial condition p(v, t = 0) = \delta(v - v_0)ϕfromtheinitialconditionp(v,t=0)=δ(v−v0​) and determine the distribution p(v, t). Give the short- and long-time limits of your result. Equation (11.83): v˙+γv=f(t)m.\dot{v} + \gamma v = \frac{f(t)}{m}.v˙+γv=mf(t)​. Equation (11.84): ∂tp+∂v(a1(v)p)−12∂v2(a2(v)p)=0.\partial_t p + \partial_v \big( a_1(v) p \big) - \tfrac{1}{2} \partial_v^2 \big( a_2(v) p \big) = 0.∂t​p+∂v​(a1​(v)p)−21​∂v2​(a2​(v)p)=0. Equation (11.85): (∂t+γk∂k+γTmk2)g(k,t)=0.\left( \partial_t + \gamma k \partial_k + \frac{\gamma T}{m} k^2 \right) g(k,t) = 0.(∂t​+γk∂k​+mγT​k2)g(k,t)=0.

Extracted Answer

(a) v(t)=v0e−γt+1m∫0tds e−γ(t−s)f(s),⟨v(t)⟩=v0e−γt,⟨v2(t)⟩=v02e−2γt+A2γm2(1−e−2γt). Since lim⁡t→∞⟨v2⟩=Tm,A=2γmT.(b) ∂t⟨v⟩=⟨a1(v)⟩,∂t⟨v2⟩=2⟨va1(v)⟩+⟨a2(v)⟩.a1(v)=−γv,a2(v)=Am2=2γTm.∂tp=γ ∂v(vp)+γTm∂v2p,(∂t+γk∂k+γTmk2)g(k,t)=0,g(k,t)=∫dv e−ikvp(v,t).(c) g(k,t)=e−Tk2/(2m)ϕ(ke−γt),g(k,0)=e−ikv0,ϕ(q)=exp⁡[−iqv0+Tq22m],g(k,t)=exp⁡[−ikv0e−γt−T2m(1−e−2γt)k2],p(v,t)=m2πT(1−e−2γt) exp⁡[−m(v−v0e−γt)22T(1−e−2γt)].t→0:p(v,t)→δ(v−v0), more precisely a narrow Gaussian with mean v0e−γt and variance Tm(1−e−2γt)≃2γTmt.t→∞:p(v,t)→m2πTexp⁡(−mv22T).\begin{gathered} \text{(a) }\quad v(t)=v_0e^{-\gamma t}+\frac1m\int_0^t ds\,e^{-\gamma(t-s)}f(s),\\ \langle v(t)\rangle=v_0e^{-\gamma t},\\ \langle v^2(t)\rangle =v_0^2e^{-2\gamma t}+\frac{A}{2\gamma m^2}\left(1-e^{-2\gamma t}\right). \text{ Since }\lim_{t\to\infty}\langle v^2\rangle=\frac{T}{m}, \quad A=2\gamma mT.\\[4pt] \text{(b) }\quad \partial_t\langle v\rangle=\langle a_1(v)\rangle,\qquad \partial_t\langle v^2\rangle=2\langle va_1(v)\rangle+\langle a_2(v)\rangle.\\ a_1(v)=-\gamma v,\qquad a_2(v)=\frac{A}{m^2}=\frac{2\gamma T}{m}.\\ \partial_t p=\gamma\,\partial_v(vp)+\frac{\gamma T}{m}\partial_v^2p,\\ \left(\partial_t+\gamma k\partial_k+\frac{\gamma T}{m}k^2\right)g(k,t)=0, \qquad g(k,t)=\int dv\,e^{-ikv}p(v,t).\\[4pt] \text{(c) }\quad g(k,t)=e^{-Tk^2/(2m)}\phi(ke^{-\gamma t}),\qquad g(k,0)=e^{-ikv_0},\\ \phi(q)=\exp\left[-iqv_0+\frac{Tq^2}{2m}\right],\\ g(k,t)=\exp\left[-ikv_0e^{-\gamma t} -\frac{T}{2m}\left(1-e^{-2\gamma t}\right)k^2\right],\\ p(v,t)= \sqrt{\frac{m}{2\pi T(1-e^{-2\gamma t})}}\, \exp\left[ -\frac{m\left(v-v_0e^{-\gamma t}\right)^2} {2T(1-e^{-2\gamma t})} \right].\\ t\to0:\quad p(v,t)\to\delta(v-v_0) \text{, more precisely a narrow Gaussian with mean }v_0e^{-\gamma t} \text{ and variance }\frac{T}{m}(1-e^{-2\gamma t})\simeq\frac{2\gamma T}{m}t.\\ t\to\infty:\quad p(v,t)\to\sqrt{\frac{m}{2\pi T}}\exp\left(-\frac{mv^2}{2T}\right). \end{gathered}(a) v(t)=v0​e−γt+m1​∫0t​dse−γ(t−s)f(s),⟨v(t)⟩=v0​e−γt,⟨v2(t)⟩=v02​e−2γt+2γm2A​(1−e−2γt). Since t→∞lim​⟨v2⟩=mT​,A=2γmT.(b) ∂t​⟨v⟩=⟨a1​(v)⟩,∂t​⟨v2⟩=2⟨va1​(v)⟩+⟨a2​(v)⟩.a1​(v)=−γv,a2​(v)=m2A​=m2γT​.∂t​p=γ∂v​(vp)+mγT​∂v2​p,(∂t​+γk∂k​+mγT​k2)g(k,t)=0,g(k,t)=∫dve−ikvp(v,t).(c) g(k,t)=e−Tk2/(2m)ϕ(ke−γt),g(k,0)=e−ikv0​,ϕ(q)=exp[−iqv0​+2mTq2​],g(k,t)=exp[−ikv0​e−γt−2mT​(1−e−2γt)k2],p(v,t)=2πT(1−e−2γt)m​​exp[−2T(1−e−2γt)m(v−v0​e−γt)2​].t→0:p(v,t)→δ(v−v0​), more precisely a narrow Gaussian with mean v0​e−γt and variance mT​(1−e−2γt)≃m2γT​t.t→∞:p(v,t)→2πTm​​exp(−2Tmv2​).​

Final Solution

(a) Integrating the equation, one obtains v(t)=e−γt[v0+∫0tdt′f(t′)meγt′].v(t) = e^{-\gamma t} \left[ v_0 + \int_0^t dt' \frac{f(t')}{m} e^{\gamma t'} \right].v(t)=e−γt[v0​+∫0t​dt′mf(t′)​eγt′]. Averaging over the Langevin force with ⟨f⟩=0gives⟨v(t)⟩=v0e−γt.\langle f \rangle = 0 gives \langle v(t) \rangle = v_0 e^{-\gamma t}.⟨f⟩=0gives⟨v(t)⟩=v0​e−γt. For the second moment, ⟨v2(t)⟩=e−2γt[v02+∫0tdt′dt′′⟨f(t′)f(t′′)⟩m2eγ(t′+t′′)]=e−2γt[v02+A2γm2(e2γt−1)].\langle v^{2}(t) \rangle = e^{-2\gamma t} \left[ v_{0}^{2} + \int_{0}^{t} dt' dt'' \frac{\langle f(t') f(t'') \rangle}{m^{2}} e^{\gamma (t' + t'')} \right] = e^{-2\gamma t} \left[ v_{0}^{2} + \frac{A}{2\gamma m^{2}} (e^{2\gamma t} - 1) \right].⟨v2(t)⟩=e−2γt[v02​+∫0t​dt′dt′′m2⟨f(t′)f(t′′)⟩​eγ(t′+t′′)]=e−2γt[v02​+2γm2A​(e2γt−1)]. In the long-time limit, ⟨v2(t)⟩→A2γm2.\langle v^{2}(t) \rangle \to \frac{A}{2 \gamma m^{2}}.⟨v2(t)⟩→2γm2A​. Equipartition, 12m⟨v2⟩=12T,thenfixesA=2γmT.\frac{1}{2} m \langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{2} T, then fixes A = 2 \gamma m T.21​m⟨v2⟩=21​T,thenfixesA=2γmT. (b) Using the Fokker–Planck equation and integrating by parts, ∂t⟨v⟩=⟨a1(v)⟩,∂t⟨v2⟩=⟨2va1(v)⟩+⟨a2(v)⟩.\partial_t \langle v \rangle = \langle a_1(v) \rangle, \\\\ \partial_t \langle v^2 \rangle = \langle 2 v a_1(v) \rangle + \langle a_2(v) \rangle.∂t​⟨v⟩=⟨a1​(v)⟩,∂t​⟨v2⟩=⟨2va1​(v)⟩+⟨a2​(v)⟩. Comparison with part (a) implies a_1(v) = −γvand⟨a2⟩=2γT/m,whichforthislinearprocessmeansa2(v)=2γT/m.-\gamma v and \langle a_2 \rangle = 2 \gamma T/m, which for this linear process means a_2(v) = 2 \gamma T/m.−γvand⟨a2​⟩=2γT/m,whichforthislinearprocessmeansa2​(v)=2γT/m. Fourier transforming p ↦g(k,t)yields\mapsto g(k,t) yields↦g(k,t)yields [∂t+γk∂k+γTmk2]g(k,t)=0,\left[ \partial_t + \gamma k \partial_k + \frac{\gamma T}{m} k^2 \right] g(k,t) = 0,[∂t​+γk∂k​+mγT​k2]g(k,t)=0, which is Eq. (11.85). (c) Method of characteristics for (11.85): characteristics satisfy d k / dt = γksok(t)=k0eγt.\gamma k so k(t) = k_0 e^{\gamma t}.γksok(t)=k0​eγt. Along them, dgdt=−γTmk2g⇒g(k,t)=exp⁡(−T2mk2)ϕ(ke−γt).\frac{d g}{d t} = - \frac{\gamma T}{m} k^2 g \Rightarrow g(k,t) = \exp\left( - \frac{T}{2m} k^2 \right) \phi(k e^{-\gamma t}).dtdg​=−mγT​k2g⇒g(k,t)=exp(−2mT​k2)ϕ(ke−γt). Initial condition p(v,0) = δ(v−v0)impliesg(k,0)=e−ikv0,henceϕ(k)=e−ikv0.\delta(v - v_0) implies g(k,0) = e^{-i k v_0}, hence \phi(k) = e^{-i k v_0}.δ(v−v0​)impliesg(k,0)=e−ikv0​,henceϕ(k)=e−ikv0​. Therefore g(k,t)=exp⁡[−ikv0e−γt−T2mk2(1−e−2γt)].g(k,t) = \exp\left[ - i k v_0 e^{-\gamma t} - \frac{T}{2m} k^2 (1 - e^{-2\gamma t}) \right].g(k,t)=exp[−ikv0​e−γt−2mT​k2(1−e−2γt)]. Inverse Fourier transform gives the Ornstein–Uhlenbeck velocity density p(v,t)=(2πTm(1−e−2γt))−1/2exp⁡[−(v−v0e−γt)22(T/m)(1−e−2γt)].p(v,t) = \left( \frac{2 \pi T}{m} (1 - e^{-2\gamma t}) \right)^{-1/2} \exp\left[ - \frac{(v - v_0 e^{-\gamma t})^2}{2 (T/m) (1 - e^{-2\gamma t})} \right].p(v,t)=(m2πT​(1−e−2γt))−1/2exp[−2(T/m)(1−e−2γt)(v−v0​e−γt)2​]. Short-time limit t ≪γ−1:aGaussiancenteredatv0withvariance2(T/m)γt\ll \gamma^{-1}: a Gaussian centered at v_0 with variance 2 (T/m) \gamma t≪γ−1:aGaussiancenteredatv0​withvariance2(T/m)γt; long-time limit t ≫γ−1:stationaryMaxwelldistributionp(v)=m/(2πT)exp⁡(−mv2/(2T)).\gg \gamma^{-1}: stationary Maxwell distribution p(v) = \sqrt{ m/(2 \pi T) } \exp(- m v^2 / (2T)).≫γ−1:stationaryMaxwelldistributionp(v)=m/(2πT)​exp(−mv2/(2T)).

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Gives the correct integrated velocity solution, mean, second moment, long-time limit, and uses equipartition to identify A = 2 gamma m T.
  • (b) Correctly states the moment equations, identifies a1(v) = -gamma v and a2(v) = 2 gamma T/m, and gives the equivalent Fokker-Planck and generating-function equations.
  • (c) Correctly applies the delta initial condition to obtain phi and g(k,t), gives the correct Gaussian Ornstein-Uhlenbeck distribution, and states the correct short- and long-time limits.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

2. textbook/12.3

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Consider a nonideal gas obeying a modified van der Waals equation of state (P+avn)(v−b)=RT(n>1).(P + \frac{a}{v^{n}})(v - b) = RT \quad (n > 1).(P+vna​)(v−b)=RT(n>1). Examine how, of this system, depend on the number nnn: (a) the critical constants PcP_cPc​, vcv_cvc​, and TcT_cTc​. (b) the critical exponents β\betaβ, γ\gammaγ, γ′\gamma'γ′, and δ\deltaδ.

Extracted Answer

(a) vc=n+1n−1 b,Tc=4naRb n−1(n−1) n−1(n+1) n+1,Pc=abn(n−1) n+1(n+1) n+1.(b) β=12,γ=1,γ′=1,δ=3.\begin{gathered} \text{(a) }\quad v_c=\frac{n+1}{n-1}\,b,\qquad T_c=\frac{4na}{R b^{\,n-1}}\frac{(n-1)^{\,n-1}}{(n+1)^{\,n+1}},\qquad P_c=\frac{a}{b^n}\frac{(n-1)^{\,n+1}}{(n+1)^{\,n+1}}.\\[4pt] \text{(b) }\quad \beta=\frac12,\qquad \gamma=1,\qquad \gamma'=1,\qquad \delta=3. \end{gathered}(a) vc​=n−1n+1​b,Tc​=Rbn−14na​(n+1)n+1(n−1)n−1​,Pc​=bna​(n+1)n+1(n−1)n+1​.(b) β=21​,γ=1,γ′=1,δ=3.​

Final Solution

The given equation of state (for one mole) of the gas is P=RTv−b−avn(n>1).(1)P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a}{v^{n}} \quad (n > 1).\tag{1}P=v−bRT​−vna​(n>1).(1) Equating (∂P/∂v)T(\partial P/\partial v)_T(∂P/∂v)T​ and (∂2P/∂v2)T(\partial^2 P/\partial v^2)_T(∂2P/∂v2)T​ to zero, we get vc=n+1n−1b,Tc=4n(n−1)n−1(n+1)n+1 abn−1R.v_c = \frac{n+1}{n-1} b, \qquad T_c = \frac{4n(n-1)^{n-1}}{(n+1)^{n+1}} \,\frac{a}{b^{n-1}R}.vc​=n−1n+1​b,Tc​=(n+1)n+14n(n−1)n−1​bn−1Ra​. Equation (1) then gives Pc=(n−1n+1)n+1abn,P_c = \left(\frac{n-1}{n+1}\right)^{n+1} \frac{a}{b^n},Pc​=(n+1n−1​)n+1bna​, whence RTcPcvc=4nn2−1\frac{R T_{c}}{P_{c}} v_{c} = \frac{4 n}{n^{2} - 1}Pc​RTc​​vc​=n2−14n​. To determine the critical behaviour, set P=Pc(1+π)P = P_c(1 + \pi)P=Pc​(1+π), v=vc(1+ψ)v = v_c(1 + \psi)v=vc​(1+ψ), T=Tc(1+t)T = T_c(1 + t)T=Tc​(1+t). A standard expansion yields π≈2nn−1t−n(n+1)212ψ3+n(n+1)n−1tψ,\pi \approx \frac{2n}{n-1}t - \frac{n(n+1)^2}{12}\psi^{3} + \frac{n(n+1)}{n-1}t\psi,π≈n−12n​t−12n(n+1)2​ψ3+n−1n(n+1)​tψ, so that along t=0t=0t=0, π∼−ψ3\pi \sim -\psi^3π∼−ψ3; for t<0t<0t<0, ψ1,3∼±∣t∣1/2\psi_{1,3} \sim \pm |t|^{1/2}ψ1,3​∼±∣t∣1/2; and −(∂ψ/∂π)t∼t−1-\left(\partial \psi/\partial \pi\right)_t \sim t^{-1}−(∂ψ/∂π)t​∼t−1 above TcT_cTc​ and ∼∣t∣−1\sim |t|^{-1}∼∣t∣−1 below along the stable branches. Thus the critical exponents are the same as for the van der Waals gas for all n>1n>1n>1: β=12,γ=1,γ′=1,δ=3,\beta = \tfrac{1}{2},\quad \gamma = 1,\quad \gamma' = 1,\quad \delta = 3,β=21​,γ=1,γ′=1,δ=3, while the critical constants depend on nnn as given above.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Gives the correct n-dependent critical constants v_c, T_c, and P_c matching the reference solution.
  • (b) Gives the correct critical exponents beta=1/2, gamma=1, gamma'=1, and delta=3 for all n>1.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

3. textbook/5.5

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

In the first approximation the partition function of a system of N noninteracting, indistinguishable particles is given by QN(V,T)=1N! λ3N ZN(V,T),Q_N(V,T) = \frac{1}{N! \, \lambda^{3N}} \, Z_N(V,T),QN​(V,T)=N!λ3N1​ZN​(V,T), where ZN(V,T)=∫exp⁡{−β∑i < jvs(rij)} d3Nr,Z_N(V,T) = \int \exp\left\{ -\beta \sum_{i \:<\: j} v_s(r_{ij}) \right\} \, d^{3N} r,ZN​(V,T)=∫exp{−βi<j∑​vs​(rij​)}d3Nr, vs(r)v_s(r)vs​(r) being the statistical potential ([5.5.28]). Evaluate the first-order correction to the equation of state of this system.

Extracted Answer

PVNkT=1∓Nλ325/2Vwith − for bosons and + for fermions, i.e. PVNkT=1−nλ325/2 for bosons, 1+nλ325/2 for fermions, n=N/V.\frac{PV}{NkT}=1\mp \frac{N\lambda^3}{2^{5/2}V} \quad\text{with }-\text{ for bosons and }+\text{ for fermions, i.e. } \frac{PV}{NkT}=1-\frac{n\lambda^3}{2^{5/2}}\text{ for bosons, } 1+\frac{n\lambda^3}{2^{5/2}}\text{ for fermions, }n=N/V.NkTPV​=1∓25/2VNλ3​with − for bosons and + for fermions, i.e. NkTPV​=1−25/2nλ3​ for bosons, 1+25/2nλ3​ for fermions, n=N/V.

Final Solution

By eqn. (5.5.17), we have QN(V,T)≡Tr(e−βH^)=1N! λ3N ZN(V,T),Q_N(V,T) \equiv \mathrm{Tr} \left( e^{-\beta \hat{H}} \right) = \frac{1}{N! \, \lambda^{3N}} \, Z_N(V,T),QN​(V,T)≡Tr(e−βH^)=N!λ3N1​ZN​(V,T), where ZN(V,T)=∫∑P{…} d3Nr.(1)Z_{N}(V,T) = \int \sum_{P} \{\ldots\} \, d^{3N}r. \tag{1}ZN​(V,T)=∫P∑​{…}d3Nr.(1) In the zeroth approximation, ∑P=1(seeeqn.\sum_{P} = 1 (see eqn.∑P​=1(seeeqn. (5.5.19)), so \; Z_N(V,T) = V^N. In the first approximation, if λismuchsmallerthanthemeaninterparticledistance,wemaywrite\lambda is much smaller than the mean interparticle distance, we may writeλismuchsmallerthanthemeaninterparticledistance,wemaywrite ∑P≈∏i<j(1±e−2πrij2/λ2)=∏i<je−βvs(rij)=exp⁡{−β∑i<jvs(rij)},(2)\sum_{P} \approx \prod_{i < j} \left( 1 \pm e^{-2\pi r_{ij}^2 / \lambda^2} \right) = \prod_{i < j} e^{-\beta v_{s}(r_{ij})} = \exp \left\{ -\beta \sum_{i < j} v_{s}(r_{ij}) \right\}, \tag{2}P∑​≈i<j∏​(1±e−2πrij2​/λ2)=i<j∏​e−βvs​(rij​)=exp{−βi<j∑​vs​(rij​)},(2) which leads directly to the desired result for Q_N in terms of Z_N. For the second part, substitute (2) into (1) and integrate over the position coordinates. On assembling contributions from all pairs of particles, one finds ZN(V,T)=VN±N(N−1)2 VN−2 λ323/2.Z_N(V,T) = V^N \pm \frac{N(N-1)}{2} \, V^{N-2} \, \frac{\lambda^{3}}{2^{3/2}}.ZN​(V,T)=VN±2N(N−1)​VN−223/2λ3​. For N ≫1andNλ3≪V,\gg 1 and N \lambda^{3} \ll V,≫1andNλ3≪V, ZN(V,T)=VN[1±N2λ325/2V]≈VN[1±Nλ325/2V]N.Z_N(V,T) = V^N \left[ 1 \pm \frac{N^2 \lambda^3}{2^{5/2} V} \right] \approx V^N \left[ 1 \pm \frac{N \lambda^3}{2^{5/2} V} \right]^N.ZN​(V,T)=VN[1±25/2VN2λ3​]≈VN[1±25/2VNλ3​]N. It follows that ln⁡QN(V,T)≈−Nln⁡N+N+Nln⁡(Vλ3)+N(±Nλ325/2V),\ln Q_N(V,T) \approx -N \ln N + N + N \ln \left(\frac{V}{\lambda^3}\right) + N \left(\pm \frac{N \lambda^3}{2^{5/2} V}\right),lnQN​(V,T)≈−NlnN+N+Nln(λ3V​)+N(±25/2VNλ3​), whence PkT≡(∂ln⁡QN∂V)N,T≈NV∓N2λ325/2V2=1v∓125/2λ3v2,\frac{P}{kT} \equiv \left(\frac{\partial \ln Q_N}{\partial V}\right)_{N,T} \approx \frac{N}{V} \mp \frac{N^2 \lambda^3}{2^{5/2} V^2} = \frac{1}{v} \mp \frac{1}{2^{5/2}} \frac{\lambda^3}{v^2},kTP​≡(∂V∂lnQN​​)N,T​≈VN​∓25/2V2N2λ3​=v1​∓25/21​v2λ3​, where v = V/N. This gives the first-order quantum-statistical correction to the ideal-gas equation of state (upper sign for bosons, lower for fermions).

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct first-order equation of state: PV/(NkT)=1 ∓ Nλ^3/(2^(5/2)V), equivalently 1 ∓ nλ^3/2^(5/2), with negative correction for bosons and positive for fermions.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

4. textbook/5.6

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

The Manning transition: A linear polyelectrolyte (rod) of length LLL and radius aaa carries NNN positive charges left on the rod, while NNN negatively charged counter-ions are in solution (water). Ignoring Coulomb repulsion among counter-ions, each counter-ion moves in the potential ϕ(r)=−2(Ne/L)ln⁡(r/L)\phi(r) = -2 (Ne/L) \ln(r/L)ϕ(r)=−2(Ne/L)ln(r/L) (cylindrical geometry), with Hamiltonian H=∑i=1N[pi22m+2e2nln⁡(r/L)],n≡N/L.\mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ \frac{p_i^{2}}{2m} + 2 e^{2} n \ln(r/L) \Big], \quad n \equiv N/L.H=i=1∑N​[2mpi2​​+2e2nln(r/L)],n≡N/L. The container is a cylinder of radius RRR and height LLL. (a) Compute the canonical partition function ZZZ in terms of T,n,R,aT,n,R,aT,n,R,a. (b) Compute the single-particle radial distribution p(r)p(r)p(r) and its first moment ⟨r⟩\langle r \rangle⟨r⟩. (c) Identify the transition temperature in the limit R≫aR \gg aR≫a. Indicate the scaling of ⟨r⟩\langle r \rangle⟨r⟩ with RRR or aaa. (d) Compute the pressure on the cylindrical wall r=Rr=Rr=R for R≫aR\gg aR≫a, all TTT. (e) Now include ion-ion repulsions approximately by allowing N1N_1N1​ ions to condense on the rod and N2=N−N1N_2=N-N_1N2​=N−N1​ to remain free with Hamiltonian ∑i=1N2[pi2/(2m)+2e2n2ln⁡(r/L)]\sum_{i=1}^{N_2} [p_i^{2}/(2m) + 2 e^{2} n_2 \ln(r/L)]∑i=1N2​​[pi2​/(2m)+2e2n2​ln(r/L)], where n2=N2/Ln_2=N_2/Ln2​=N2​/L. Guess the equilibrium N2∗N_2^{*}N2∗​.

Extracted Answer

λT=h2πmkBT,q=e2nkBT,N=nL.(a)   Z=1N![2πL3λT3Aq]N,Aq={(R/L)2(1−q)−(a/L)2(1−q)2(1−q),q≠1,ln⁡(R/a),q=1.(b)   p(r)=r1−2q∫aRr′ 1−2q dr′,a≤r≤R,p(r)={2(1−q)r1−2qR2−2q−a2−2q,q≠1,1rln⁡(R/a),q=1,⟨r⟩={2(1−q)3−2qR3−2q−a3−2qR2−2q−a2−2q,q≠1,  q≠3/2,R−aln⁡(R/a),q=1,aRR−aln⁡(R/a),q=3/2.(c)   Tc=e2nkB  (q=1). For R≫a:⟨r⟩∼{R,T>Tc,R/ln⁡(R/a),T=Tc,a(R/a)3−2q,2Tc/3<T<Tc,aln⁡(R/a),T=2Tc/3,a,T<2Tc/3.(d)   P=kBT2πRL∂ln⁡Z∂R∼{nkBT(1−q)πR2,q<1,nkBT2πR2ln⁡(R/a),q=1,nkBT(q−1)πR2(aR)2q−2,q>1.(e)   N2∗=min⁡ ⁣(N,LkBTe2),N1∗=N−N2∗, i.e. the free ions self-adjust to e2(N2∗/L)/(kBT)=1 when condensed.\begin{gathered} \lambda_T=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_BT}},\qquad q=\frac{e^2 n}{k_BT},\qquad N=nL.\\[1mm] \text{(a) }\; Z=\frac{1}{N!}\left[\frac{2\pi L^3}{\lambda_T^3}A_q\right]^N,\quad A_q=\begin{cases} \dfrac{(R/L)^{2(1-q)}-(a/L)^{2(1-q)}}{2(1-q)},&q\ne1,\\[1mm] \ln(R/a),&q=1. \end{cases}\\[2mm] \text{(b) }\; p(r)=\frac{r^{1-2q}}{\int_a^R r'^{\,1-2q}\,dr'},\quad a\le r\le R,\\ p(r)=\begin{cases} \dfrac{2(1-q)r^{1-2q}}{R^{2-2q}-a^{2-2q}},&q\ne1,\\[1mm] \dfrac{1}{r\ln(R/a)},&q=1, \end{cases}\\ \langle r\rangle=\begin{cases} \dfrac{2(1-q)}{3-2q}\dfrac{R^{3-2q}-a^{3-2q}}{R^{2-2q}-a^{2-2q}},&q\ne1,\;q\ne3/2,\\[2mm] \dfrac{R-a}{\ln(R/a)},&q=1,\\[1mm] \dfrac{aR}{R-a}\ln(R/a),&q=3/2. \end{cases}\\[2mm] \text{(c) }\; T_c=\dfrac{e^2n}{k_B}\;(q=1). \text{ For }R\gg a:\quad \langle r\rangle\sim \begin{cases} R,&T>T_c,\\ R/\ln(R/a),&T=T_c,\\ a(R/a)^{3-2q},&2T_c/3<T<T_c,\\ a\ln(R/a),&T=2T_c/3,\\ a,&T<2T_c/3. \end{cases}\\[2mm] \text{(d) }\; P=\frac{k_BT}{2\pi RL}\frac{\partial\ln Z}{\partial R} \sim \begin{cases} \dfrac{n k_BT(1-q)}{\pi R^2},&q<1,\\[2mm] \dfrac{n k_BT}{2\pi R^2\ln(R/a)},&q=1,\\[2mm] \dfrac{n k_BT(q-1)}{\pi R^2}\left(\dfrac{a}{R}\right)^{2q-2},&q>1. \end{cases}\\[2mm] \text{(e) }\; N_2^*=\min\!\left(N,\frac{Lk_BT}{e^2}\right),\qquad N_1^*=N-N_2^*, \text{ i.e. the free ions self-adjust to }e^2(N_2^*/L)/(k_BT)=1\text{ when condensed.} \end{gathered}λT​=2πmkB​T​h​,q=kB​Te2n​,N=nL.(a) Z=N!1​[λT3​2πL3​Aq​]N,Aq​=⎩⎨⎧​2(1−q)(R/L)2(1−q)−(a/L)2(1−q)​,ln(R/a),​q=1,q=1.​(b) p(r)=∫aR​r′1−2qdr′r1−2q​,a≤r≤R,p(r)=⎩⎨⎧​R2−2q−a2−2q2(1−q)r1−2q​,rln(R/a)1​,​q=1,q=1,​⟨r⟩=⎩⎨⎧​3−2q2(1−q)​R2−2q−a2−2qR3−2q−a3−2q​,ln(R/a)R−a​,R−aaR​ln(R/a),​q=1,q=3/2,q=1,q=3/2.​(c) Tc​=kB​e2n​(q=1). For R≫a:⟨r⟩∼⎩⎨⎧​R,R/ln(R/a),a(R/a)3−2q,aln(R/a),a,​T>Tc​,T=Tc​,2Tc​/3<T<Tc​,T=2Tc​/3,T<2Tc​/3.​(d) P=2πRLkB​T​∂R∂lnZ​∼⎩⎨⎧​πR2nkB​T(1−q)​,2πR2ln(R/a)nkB​T​,πR2nkB​T(q−1)​(Ra​)2q−2,​q<1,q=1,q>1.​(e) N2∗​=min(N,e2LkB​T​),N1∗​=N−N2∗​, i.e. the free ions self-adjust to e2(N2∗​/L)/(kB​T)=1 when condensed.​

Final Solution

(a) Each particle contributes a momentum factor λ−3\lambda^{-3}λ−3 with λ=h/2πmkBT\lambda=h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}λ=h/2πmkB​T​. In cylindrical coordinates, the spatial integral per particle gives ∫aR(2πL)r dr (r/L)−2e2n/(kBT)\int_{a}^{R} (2\pi L) r \, dr\, (r/L)^{-2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)}∫aR​(2πL)rdr(r/L)−2e2n/(kB​T). Hence Z=1N!(1λ3∫aR2πL r dr (r/L)−2e2n/(kBT)) ⁣N=(2πen λ3) ⁣NL2Ne2n/(kBT)[R2(1−e2n/(kBT))−a2(1−e2n/(kBT))2(1−e2n/(kBT))] ⁣N.Z = \frac{1}{N!}\Big( \frac{1}{\lambda^{3}} \int_{a}^{R} 2\pi L \, r\, dr\, (r/L)^{-2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)} \Big)^{\!N} = \Big( \frac{2\pi e}{n\, \lambda^{3}} \Big)^{\!N} L^{2 N e^{2} n/(k_B T)} \Big[ \frac{R^{2(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T))} - a^{2(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T))}}{2\big(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T)\big)} \Big]^{\!N}.Z=N!1​(λ31​∫aR​2πLrdr(r/L)−2e2n/(kB​T))N=(nλ32πe​)NL2Ne2n/(kB​T)[2(1−e2n/(kB​T))R2(1−e2n/(kB​T))−a2(1−e2n/(kB​T))​]N. (up to overall constants not affecting thermodynamics in (c,d)). (b) The one-body distribution is p(r)=r e−[2e2n/(kBT)]ln⁡(r/L)∫aRdr r e−[2e2n/(kBT)]ln⁡(r/L)=2(1−e2nkBT)r1−2e2n/(kBT)R2(1−e2n/(kBT))−a2(1−e2n/(kBT)),p(r) = \frac{r\, e^{-[2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)] \ln(r/L)}}{\int_{a}^{R} dr\, r\, e^{-[2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)] \ln(r/L)}} = 2\Big(1 - \frac{e^{2} n}{k_B T}\Big) \frac{r^{1 - 2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)}}{R^{2(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T))} - a^{2(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T))}},p(r)=∫aR​drre−[2e2n/(kB​T)]ln(r/L)re−[2e2n/(kB​T)]ln(r/L)​=2(1−kB​Te2n​)R2(1−e2n/(kB​T))−a2(1−e2n/(kB​T))r1−2e2n/(kB​T)​, normalized on [a,R][a,R][a,R]. The mean radius is ⟨r⟩=∫aRdr r p(r)=2kBT−2e2n3kBT−2e2n R3−2e2n/(kBT)−a3−2e2n/(kBT)R2−2e2n/(kBT)−a2−2e2n/(kBT).\langle r \rangle = \int_{a}^{R} dr\, r\, p(r) = \frac{2k_B T - 2 e^{2} n}{3k_B T - 2 e^{2} n} \, \frac{R^{3 - 2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)} - a^{3 - 2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)}}{R^{2 - 2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)} - a^{2 - 2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)}}.⟨r⟩=∫aR​drrp(r)=3kB​T−2e2n2kB​T−2e2n​R2−2e2n/(kB​T)−a2−2e2n/(kB​T)R3−2e2n/(kB​T)−a3−2e2n/(kB​T)​. (c) For R≫aR\gg aR≫a: If e2n/(kBT)>1e^{2} n/(k_B T) > 1e2n/(kB​T)>1 (low TTT), p(r)∝r1−2e2n/(kBT)p(r)\propto r^{1-2 e^{2} n/(k_B T)}p(r)∝r1−2e2n/(kB​T) is normalizable as R→∞R\to\inftyR→∞ and ⟨r⟩∝a\langle r \rangle \propto a⟨r⟩∝a (condensed on the rod). If e2n/(kBT)<1e^{2} n/(k_B T) < 1e2n/(kB​T)<1 (high TTT), ⟨r⟩∝R\langle r \rangle \propto R⟨r⟩∝R (counter-ions spread in the container). The transition is at Tc=e2nkB.T_c = \frac{e^{2} n}{k_B}.Tc​=kB​e2n​. (d) The wall pressure follows from P=(kBT)/(2πRL) ∂ln⁡Z/∂RP = (k_B T)/(2\pi R L) \, \partial \ln Z/\partial RP=(kB​T)/(2πRL)∂lnZ/∂R. For T<TcT<T_cT<Tc​, ZZZ becomes RRR-independent in the R≫aR\gg aR≫a limit, so P=0P=0P=0. For T>TcT>T_cT>Tc​, one finds P=kBT2πRL⋅2N(1−e2n/(kBT))R,P = \frac{k_B T}{2\pi R L} \cdot \frac{2N\big(1 - e^{2} n/(k_B T)\big)}{R},P=2πRLkB​T​⋅R2N(1−e2n/(kB​T))​, so equivalently PV=NkBT(1−e2nkBT),V=πR2L.P V = N k_B T \Big(1 - \frac{e^{2} n}{k_B T}\Big), \quad V=\pi R^{2} L.PV=NkB​T(1−kB​Te2n​),V=πR2L. (e) The non-interacting free ions behave as in (a)-(d) with n→n2n\to n_2n→n2​. Stability requires the effective control parameter be at the critical value, hence n2∗=kBTe2⇒N2∗=kBTe2 L.n_2^{*} = \frac{k_B T}{e^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_2^{*} = \frac{k_B T}{e^{2}} \, L.n2∗​=e2kB​T​⇒N2∗​=e2kB​T​L. If n2>n2∗n_2 > n_2^{*}n2​>n2∗​ at T<TcT<T_cT<Tc​, ions condense to reduce n2n_2n2​; if n2<n2∗n_2 < n_2^{*}n2​<n2∗​, ions evaporate to increase n2n_2n2​. For T>TcT>T_cT>Tc​, n2∗=nn_2^{*}=nn2∗​=n and there is no condensation.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct partition function, including the thermal wavelength, indistinguishability factor, cylindrical spatial integral, and the logarithmic critical case q=1.
  • (b) Correct normalized radial distribution and first moment, with correct limiting forms at q=1 and q=3/2.
  • (c) Correct transition temperature T_c=e^2 n/k_B and correct R/a scaling from the stated mean-radius formula, including the refined subregimes below T_c.
  • (d) Correct wall-pressure expression from the R derivative of ln Z. It matches the reference above T_c, vanishes asymptotically below T_c, and gives the critical logarithmic limit.
  • (e) Correct equilibrium free-ion number, including saturation at N for T>T_c and condensation to N_2^*=L k_B T/e^2 when below the transition.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

5. kinetic/3.5

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Two dimensional electron gas in a magnetic field: Consider a classical gas of electrons confined to two dimensions in a uniform magnetic field B⃗\vec BB. (a) For non-interacting electrons with Hamiltonian H=∑i[(p⃗i−eA⃗)2/(2m)±μB∣B⃗∣]\mathcal H = \sum_i \big[(\vec p_i - e\vec A)^2/(2m) \pm \mu_B |\vec B|\big]H=∑i​[(p​i​−eA)2/(2m)±μB​∣B∣] with A⃗=(B⃗×q⃗)/2\vec A= (\vec B\times \vec q)/2A=(B×q​)/2, obtain the classical equations of motion. (b) Write the Boltzmann equations for the densities f↑(p,q,t)f_{\uparrow}(\mathbf p,\mathbf q,t)f↑​(p,q,t) and f↓(p,q,t)f_{\downarrow}(\mathbf p,\mathbf q,t)f↓​(p,q,t) of electrons with up and down spins, in terms of the two cross-sections σ≡σ↑↑=σ↓↓\sigma\equiv\sigma_{\uparrow\uparrow}=\sigma_{\downarrow\downarrow}σ≡σ↑↑​=σ↓↓​, and σ×≡σ↑↓\sigma_\times\equiv\sigma_{\uparrow\downarrow}σ×​≡σ↑↓​ for spin-conserving collisions. (g) Write down the most general form for the equilibrium distribution functions for particles confined to a circularly symmetric potential.

Extracted Answer

Let π=p−eA(q),v=π/m,ϵ↑,↓=±μB∣B∣,↑ˉ=↓, ↓ˉ=↑.(a) For each particle,q˙=p−eAm,p˙i=e q˙j ∂iAj,mv˙=e v×B.For B=Bz^, A=(−By/2,Bx/2), this is x˙=px+eBy/2m,y˙=py−eBx/2m,p˙x=eB2y˙,p˙y=−eB2x˙,equivalently mx¨=eBy˙,my¨=−eBx˙. The constant Zeeman terms ϵs do not affect the orbit.(b) The Boltzmann equations are[∂t+q˙⋅∇q+p˙⋅∇p]fs(p,q,t)=Cs[f↑,f↓],s=↑,↓,C↑=I↑↑σ+I↑↓σ×,C↓=I↓↓σ+I↓↑σ×,Isrκ(p1)=∫d2p2∫dθ g κ(g,θ)[fs(p1′)fr(p2′)−fs(p1)fr(p2)],g=∣(p1−eA)−(p2−eA)∣/m,p1′,p2′ are the pre-collision momenta giving p1,p2 by an elastic spin-conserving collision.(g) With a circular potential U(r), define Hs=(p−eA)22m+U(r)+ϵs,Lz=(q×p)z.Collisionless stationary distributions may be any Fs(Hs,Lz). For Boltzmann equilibrium with opposite-spin collisions present,fseq(p,q)=Asexp⁡ ⁣[−β((p−eA)22m+U(r)+ϵs−ΩLz)],s=↑,↓,where A↑,A↓ are independent normalizations/chemical potentials, while β and Ω are common to both spin species, with parameters chosen so the distribution is normalizable.\begin{gathered} \text{Let } \boldsymbol{\pi}=\mathbf p-e\mathbf A(\mathbf q),\quad \mathbf v=\boldsymbol{\pi}/m,\quad \epsilon_{\uparrow,\downarrow}=\pm \mu_B|\mathbf B|,\quad \bar\uparrow=\downarrow,\ \bar\downarrow=\uparrow. \\[2mm] \text{(a) For each particle,} \qquad \dot{\mathbf q}=\frac{\mathbf p-e\mathbf A}{m},\qquad \dot p_i=e\,\dot q_j\,\partial_i A_j,\qquad m\dot{\mathbf v}=e\,\mathbf v\times\mathbf B . \\ \text{For } \mathbf B=B\hat{\mathbf z},\ \mathbf A=(-By/2,Bx/2),\text{ this is } \dot x=\frac{p_x+eBy/2}{m},\quad \dot y=\frac{p_y-eBx/2}{m},\quad \dot p_x=\frac{eB}{2}\dot y,\quad \dot p_y=-\frac{eB}{2}\dot x, \\ \text{equivalently } m\ddot x=eB\dot y,\quad m\ddot y=-eB\dot x. \text{ The constant Zeeman terms } \epsilon_s \text{ do not affect the orbit.} \\[2mm] \text{(b) The Boltzmann equations are} \\ \left[ \partial_t+\dot{\mathbf q}\cdot\nabla_{\mathbf q}+\dot{\mathbf p}\cdot\nabla_{\mathbf p} \right]f_s(\mathbf p,\mathbf q,t)=C_s[f_\uparrow,f_\downarrow], \qquad s=\uparrow,\downarrow, \\ C_\uparrow=\mathcal I_{\uparrow\uparrow}^{\sigma}+\mathcal I_{\uparrow\downarrow}^{\sigma_\times}, \qquad C_\downarrow=\mathcal I_{\downarrow\downarrow}^{\sigma}+\mathcal I_{\downarrow\uparrow}^{\sigma_\times}, \\ \mathcal I_{sr}^{\kappa}(\mathbf p_1) = \int d^2p_2\int d\theta\, g\,\kappa(g,\theta) \left[ f_s(\mathbf p_1')f_r(\mathbf p_2')- f_s(\mathbf p_1)f_r(\mathbf p_2) \right], \\ g=\left|(\mathbf p_1-e\mathbf A)-(\mathbf p_2-e\mathbf A)\right|/m, \quad \mathbf p_1',\mathbf p_2' \text{ are the pre-collision momenta giving } \mathbf p_1,\mathbf p_2 \text{ by an elastic spin-conserving collision.} \\[2mm] \text{(g) With a circular potential }U(r),\text{ define } H_s=\frac{(\mathbf p-e\mathbf A)^2}{2m}+U(r)+\epsilon_s, \qquad L_z=(\mathbf q\times\mathbf p)_z . \\ \text{Collisionless stationary distributions may be any }F_s(H_s,L_z). \text{ For Boltzmann equilibrium with opposite-spin collisions present,} \\ f_s^{\rm eq}(\mathbf p,\mathbf q) = A_s\exp\!\left[-\beta\left( \frac{(\mathbf p-e\mathbf A)^2}{2m}+U(r)+\epsilon_s-\Omega L_z \right)\right], \qquad s=\uparrow,\downarrow, \\ \text{where }A_\uparrow,A_\downarrow\text{ are independent normalizations/chemical potentials, while } \beta\text{ and }\Omega\text{ are common to both spin species, with parameters chosen so the distribution is normalizable.} \end{gathered}Let π=p−eA(q),v=π/m,ϵ↑,↓​=±μB​∣B∣,↑ˉ​=↓, ↓ˉ​=↑.(a) For each particle,q˙​=mp−eA​,p˙​i​=eq˙​j​∂i​Aj​,mv˙=ev×B.For B=Bz^, A=(−By/2,Bx/2), this is x˙=mpx​+eBy/2​,y˙​=mpy​−eBx/2​,p˙​x​=2eB​y˙​,p˙​y​=−2eB​x˙,equivalently mx¨=eBy˙​,my¨​=−eBx˙. The constant Zeeman terms ϵs​ do not affect the orbit.(b) The Boltzmann equations are[∂t​+q˙​⋅∇q​+p˙​⋅∇p​]fs​(p,q,t)=Cs​[f↑​,f↓​],s=↑,↓,C↑​=I↑↑σ​+I↑↓σ×​​,C↓​=I↓↓σ​+I↓↑σ×​​,Isrκ​(p1​)=∫d2p2​∫dθgκ(g,θ)[fs​(p1′​)fr​(p2′​)−fs​(p1​)fr​(p2​)],g=∣(p1​−eA)−(p2​−eA)∣/m,p1′​,p2′​ are the pre-collision momenta giving p1​,p2​ by an elastic spin-conserving collision.(g) With a circular potential U(r), define Hs​=2m(p−eA)2​+U(r)+ϵs​,Lz​=(q×p)z​.Collisionless stationary distributions may be any Fs​(Hs​,Lz​). For Boltzmann equilibrium with opposite-spin collisions present,fseq​(p,q)=As​exp[−β(2m(p−eA)2​+U(r)+ϵs​−ΩLz​)],s=↑,↓,where A↑​,A↓​ are independent normalizations/chemical potentials, while β and Ω are common to both spin species, with parameters chosen so the distribution is normalizable.​

Final Solution

(a) With A⃗=(B⃗×q⃗)/2\vec A=(\vec B\times\vec q)/2A=(B×q​)/2, Hamilton's equations yield q⃗˙=∂H/∂p⃗=(p⃗−eA⃗)/m\dot{\vec q}=\partial \mathcal H/\partial \vec p = (\vec p - e\vec A)/mq​˙​=∂H/∂p​=(p​−eA)/m, and p⃗˙=−∂H/∂q⃗=(e/m)(p⃗−eA⃗)×B⃗/2+⋯\dot{\vec p}= -\partial \mathcal H/\partial \vec q = (e/m)(\vec p - e\vec A)\times\vec B/2 + \cdotsp​˙​=−∂H/∂q​=(e/m)(p​−eA)×B/2+⋯. Eliminating p⃗\vec pp​ gives mq⃗¨=e q⃗˙×B⃗m\ddot{\vec q} = e\, \dot{\vec q}\times \vec Bmq​¨​=eq​˙​×B, i.e. cyclotron motion in planes perpendicular to B⃗\vec BB with ω⃗c=(e/m)B⃗\vec\omega_c = (e/m)\vec Bωc​=(e/m)B. (b) The Boltzmann equations with elastic spin-conserving collisions are ∂tf↑−{H↑,f↑}=∫d2p2 dΩ ∣v1−v2∣[(dσ/dΩ)(f↑′f↑′2−f↑f↑2)+(dσ×/dΩ)(f↑′f↓′−f↑f↓)]\partial_t f_\uparrow - \{\mathcal H_\uparrow, f_\uparrow\} = \int d^2p_2\, d\Omega\, |v_1-v_2|\big[ (d\sigma/d\Omega)(f_\uparrow'f_\uparrow^{\prime 2} - f_\uparrow f_\uparrow^2) + (d\sigma_\times/d\Omega)(f_\uparrow'f_\downarrow' - f_\uparrow f_\downarrow)\big]∂t​f↑​−{H↑​,f↑​}=∫d2p2​dΩ∣v1​−v2​∣[(dσ/dΩ)(f↑′​f↑′2​−f↑​f↑2​)+(dσ×​/dΩ)(f↑′​f↓′​−f↑​f↓​)], and similarly for ↓\downarrow↓ with ↑↔↓\uparrow\leftrightarrow\downarrow↑↔↓. (c) Define H=∫d2q d2p [f↑ln⁡f↑+f↓ln⁡f↓]H=\int d^2q\,d^2p\, [f_\uparrow\ln f_\uparrow + f_\downarrow\ln f_\downarrow]H=∫d2qd2p[f↑​lnf↑​+f↓​lnf↓​]. Using the standard symmetrization of the collision integrals, one finds each contribution is non-positive, hence dH/dt≤0dH/dt\le 0dH/dt≤0. (d) If at each q⃗\vec qq​, ln⁡fα(p⃗)\ln f_\alpha(\vec p)lnfα​(p​) is a linear combination of collision invariants, then for every binary collision the sums of ln⁡f\ln flnf before and after are equal, making each collision contribution vanish; thus dH/dt=0dH/dt=0dH/dt=0. (e) If fα=fα(I1,I2,… )f_\alpha=f_\alpha(I_1,I_2,\dots)fα​=fα​(I1​,I2​,…) depends only on invariants IjI_jIj​ with {Ij,Hα}=0\{I_j,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=0{Ij​,Hα​}=0, then the streaming term vanishes: {fα,Hα}=∑j(∂fα/∂Ij){Ij,Hα}=0\{f_\alpha,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=\sum_j (\partial f_\alpha/\partial I_j)\{I_j,\mathcal H_\alpha\}=0{fα​,Hα​}=∑j​(∂fα​/∂Ij​){Ij​,Hα​}=0. (f) During collisions at fixed q⃗\vec qq​, momentum conservation implies q⃗×(p⃗1+p⃗2)=q⃗×(p⃗1′+p⃗2′)\vec q\times(\vec p_1+\vec p_2)=\vec q\times(\vec p'_1+\vec p'_2)q​×(p​1​+p​2​)=q​×(p​1′​+p​2′​) so L⃗\vec LL is conserved. Away from collisions, for the given H\mathcal HH, {H,q⃗×p⃗}=0\{\mathcal H,\vec q\times\vec p\}=0{H,q​×p​}=0; in 2D the conserved component is LzL_zLz​. (g) The most general equilibrium form compatible with (e) and collisions is fα=Aαexp⁡[−βHα−γLz]f_\alpha = A_\alpha \exp[-\beta \mathcal H_\alpha - \gamma L_z]fα​=Aα​exp[−βHα​−γLz​] (possible rigid rotation via γ\gammaγ). (h) Impurities break momentum (and LzL_zLz​) conservation, removing the γ\gammaγ term. Magnetic impurities mix spins implying A↑=A↓A_\uparrow=A_\downarrowA↑​=A↓​; non-magnetic do not. (i) Angular momentum conservation does not yield an independent hydrodynamic equation beyond momentum; spin conservation yields a new equation for magnetization (spin density difference).

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct Hamilton equations are given, including the Lorentz-force form after eliminating canonical momentum and the fact that the constant Zeeman term does not affect the orbital motion.
  • (b) The Boltzmann equations include the correct streaming term and collision integrals for same-spin and opposite-spin elastic spin-conserving collisions with the two cross sections, in an equivalent notation.
  • (g) The equilibrium distribution is given as a Maxwell-Boltzmann exponential with common beta and rigid-rotation angular momentum term, with spin-dependent normalizations, matching the reference form up to the equivalent convention gamma = -beta Omega and including the circular potential.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

6. probability/2.5

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

5. Benford's law describes the observed probabilities of the first digit in a great variety of data sets, such as stock prices. Rather counter-intuitively, the digits 1 through 9 occur with probabilities 0.301, 0.176, 0.125, 0.097, 0.079, 0.067, 0.058, 0.051, 0.046 respectively. The key observation is that this distribution is invariant under a change of scale. Find a formula that fits the above probabilities on the basis of this observation.

Extracted Answer

For first digit d=1,…,9,P(d)=log⁡10 ⁣(d+1d)=log⁡10 ⁣(1+1d), giving (0.3010,0.1761,0.1249,0.0969,0.0792,0.0669,0.0580,0.0512,0.0458) for d=1,…,9.\text{For first digit }d=1,\ldots,9,\quad P(d)=\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d+1}{d}\right)=\log_{10}\!\left(1+\frac1d\right),\text{ giving }(0.3010,0.1761,0.1249,0.0969,0.0792,0.0669,0.0580,0.0512,0.0458)\text{ for }d=1,\ldots,9.For first digit d=1,…,9,P(d)=log10​(dd+1​)=log10​(1+d1​), giving (0.3010,0.1761,0.1249,0.0969,0.0792,0.0669,0.0580,0.0512,0.0458) for d=1,…,9.

Final Solution

Consider products x=∏i=1Nriwithpositiverandomri.x=\prod_{i=1}^N r_i with positive random r_i.x=∏i=1N​ri​withpositiverandomri​. Then ℓ=ln⁡x=∑ln⁡ri\ell=\ln x=\sum\ln r_iℓ=lnx=∑lnri​ is Gaussian for large N (CLT), so x is log-normal: p(x)∝x−1exp⁡[−(ln⁡x−μ)2/(2σ2)]p(x)\propto x^{-1}\exp[-(\ln x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)]p(x)∝x−1exp[−(lnx−μ)2/(2σ2)]. The probability that the first digit is i is pi≈∑q∫10qi10q(i+1)p(x) dx=∑q∫q+ln⁡iq+ln⁡(i+1)p(ℓ) dℓ∝ln⁡(i+1)−ln⁡i=ln⁡(1+1i).p_i\approx \sum_q\int_{10^q i}^{10^q(i+1)} p(x)\,dx=\sum_q\int_{q+\ln i}^{q+\ln(i+1)} p(\ell)\,d\ell\propto \ln(i+1)-\ln i=\ln\left(1+\frac{1}{i}\right).pi​≈q∑​∫10qi10q(i+1)​p(x)dx=q∑​∫q+lniq+ln(i+1)​p(ℓ)dℓ∝ln(i+1)−lni=ln(1+i1​). Normalizing in base 10 yields Benford's law pi=log⁡10 ⁣(1+1i),i=1,…,9,p_i=\log_{10}\!\left(1+\frac{1}{i}\right),\quad i=1,\dots,9,pi​=log10​(1+i1​),i=1,…,9, which matches the listed frequencies.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) The candidate gives the Benford formula P(d)=log10((d+1)/d)=log10(1+1/d) for d=1,...,9 and the corresponding probabilities, matching the reference solution.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

7. textbook/3.8.9

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

3.8.9 Pauli paramagnetism Consider electrons with Zeeman coupling H^z=−μ0B⋅S^\hat{H}_z=-\mu_0 \mathbf{B}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{S}}H^z​=−μ0​B⋅S^, S^=12aασ† σσσ′aασ′\hat{\mathbf{S}}=\tfrac{1}{2} a^{\dagger}_{\alpha\sigma}\,\boldsymbol{\sigma}_{\sigma\sigma'} a_{\alpha\sigma'}S^=21​aασ†​σσσ′​aασ′​, and a non-magnetic part H^0=∑α,σaασ† ϵα aασ\hat{H}_0=\sum_{\alpha,\sigma} a^{\dagger}_{\alpha\sigma}\,\epsilon_{\alpha}\,a_{\alpha\sigma}H^0​=∑α,σ​aασ†​ϵα​aασ​. A magnetic field lifts spin degeneracy near the Fermi surface and produces a Pauli paramagnetic response. (a) Construct the coherent-state action of H^=H^0+H^z\hat{H}=\hat{H}_0+\hat{H}_zH^=H^0​+H^z​. Integrate out the Grassmann fields to obtain the free energy FFF as a sum over Matsubara frequencies. (b) Determine the low-temperature spin contribution to the susceptibility χ≡−∂B2F∣B=0\chi\equiv -\left.\partial_B^2 F\right|_{B=0}χ≡−∂B2​F​B=0​, where ρ(ϵ)=∑αδ(ϵ−ϵα)\rho(\epsilon)=\sum_{\alpha}\delta(\epsilon-\epsilon_{\alpha})ρ(ϵ)=∑α​δ(ϵ−ϵα​) is the single-particle density of states.

Extracted Answer

(a)S=∫0βdτ ψˉασ[(∂τ+ϵα−μ)δσσ′−μ02B⋅σσσ′]ψασ′,F=−T∑α,nln⁡det⁡[(−iωn+ϵα−μ)1−μ02B⋅σ]=−T∑α,nln⁡[(−iωn+ϵα−μ)2−(μ0B2)2]up to a B-independent constant;(b)χ=μ022∫dϵ ρ(ϵ)[−∂nF(ϵ−μ)∂ϵ]=μ022[ρ(μ)+π2T26ρ′′(μ)+O(T4)],hence at T=0,χ=μ022ρ(ϵF).\begin{aligned} \text{(a)}\quad &S=\int_0^\beta d\tau\, \bar\psi_{\alpha\sigma} \left[ (\partial_\tau+\epsilon_\alpha-\mu)\delta_{\sigma\sigma'} -\frac{\mu_0}{2}\mathbf B\cdot\boldsymbol\sigma_{\sigma\sigma'} \right]\psi_{\alpha\sigma'},\\ &F=-T\sum_{\alpha,n}\ln\det\left[ (-i\omega_n+\epsilon_\alpha-\mu)\mathbf 1 -\frac{\mu_0}{2}\mathbf B\cdot\boldsymbol\sigma \right]\\ &\hspace{1.35cm} =-T\sum_{\alpha,n}\ln\left[ (-i\omega_n+\epsilon_\alpha-\mu)^2-\left(\frac{\mu_0B}{2}\right)^2 \right] \quad \text{up to a \(B\)-independent constant};\\[1ex] \text{(b)}\quad &\chi= \frac{\mu_0^2}{2}\int d\epsilon\,\rho(\epsilon) \left[-\frac{\partial n_F(\epsilon-\mu)}{\partial\epsilon}\right] = \frac{\mu_0^2}{2}\left[ \rho(\mu)+\frac{\pi^2T^2}{6}\rho''(\mu)+O(T^4) \right],\\ &\text{hence at }T=0,\qquad \chi=\frac{\mu_0^2}{2}\rho(\epsilon_F). \end{aligned}(a)(b)​S=∫0β​dτψˉ​ασ​[(∂τ​+ϵα​−μ)δσσ′​−2μ0​​B⋅σσσ′​]ψασ′​,F=−Tα,n∑​lndet[(−iωn​+ϵα​−μ)1−2μ0​​B⋅σ]=−Tα,n∑​ln[(−iωn​+ϵα​−μ)2−(2μ0​B​)2]up to a B-independent constant;χ=2μ02​​∫dϵρ(ϵ)[−∂ϵ∂nF​(ϵ−μ)​]=2μ02​​[ρ(μ)+6π2T2​ρ′′(μ)+O(T4)],hence at T=0,χ=2μ02​​ρ(ϵF​).​

Final Solution

(a) Choosing the field along the quantization axis, H^=∑ασaασ†(ϵα−μ0B2(σz)σσ)aασ\hat{H}=\sum_{\alpha\sigma} a^{\dagger}_{\alpha\sigma}(\epsilon_{\alpha}-\tfrac{\mu_0 B}{2}(\sigma_z)_{\sigma\sigma})a_{\alpha\sigma}H^=∑ασ​aασ†​(ϵα​−2μ0​B​(σz​)σσ​)aασ​. The action in frequency space is S=∑ασnψˉασn(−iωn+ξα−μ0B2(σz)σσ)ψασnS=\sum_{\alpha\sigma n}\bar{\psi}_{\alpha\sigma n}(-i\omega_n+\xi_{\alpha}-\tfrac{\mu_0 B}{2}(\sigma_z)_{\sigma\sigma})\psi_{\alpha\sigma n}S=∑ασn​ψˉ​ασn​(−iωn​+ξα​−2μ0​B​(σz​)σσ​)ψασn​. Integrating out ψ\psiψ gives F=−T∑αnln⁡(β2[(−iωn+ξα)2−14(μ0B)2]).F=-T\sum_{\alpha n}\ln\Big(\beta^2\big[(-i\omega_n+\xi_{\alpha})^2-\tfrac{1}{4}(\mu_0 B)^2\big]\Big).F=−Tαn∑​ln(β2[(−iωn​+ξα​)2−41​(μ0​B)2]). (b) Differentiating twice and setting B=0B=0B=0 yields χ=−12μ02T∑αn(−iωn+ξα)−2\chi=-\tfrac{1}{2}\mu_0^2 T\sum_{\alpha n}(-i\omega_n+\xi_{\alpha})^{-2}χ=−21​μ02​T∑αn​(−iωn​+ξα​)−2. Performing the Matsubara sum using residues gives χ=−(μ02/2)∑αnF′(ξα)=−(μ02/2)∫dϵ ρ(ϵ)nF′(ϵ−μ)\chi=-(\mu_0^2/2)\sum_{\alpha} n_F'(\xi_{\alpha})=-(\mu_0^2/2)\int d\epsilon\,\rho(\epsilon) n_F'(\epsilon-\mu)χ=−(μ02​/2)∑α​nF′​(ξα​)=−(μ02​/2)∫dϵρ(ϵ)nF′​(ϵ−μ). As T→0T\to 0T→0, nF′(ϵ−μ)→−δ(ϵ−μ)n_F'(\epsilon-\mu)\to -\delta(\epsilon-\mu)nF′​(ϵ−μ)→−δ(ϵ−μ), hence χ→(μ02/2)ρ(μ)\chi\to (\mu_0^2/2)\rho(\mu)χ→(μ02​/2)ρ(μ).

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly gives the coherent-state action with chemical potential included as xi = epsilon_alpha - mu, includes the Zeeman matrix term, and integrates out the Grassmann fields to obtain the Matsubara log determinant/free energy. The missing beta factor is explicitly only a B-independent constant, consistent with the reference.
  • (b) Correctly obtains the spin susceptibility as (mu0^2/2) integral rho(epsilon)(-partial n_F/partial epsilon), and gives the T=0 limit (mu0^2/2) rho(mu). The Sommerfeld correction is consistent and does not introduce a material error.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

8. textbook/5.4

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Two dimensional Coulomb gas: Consider a classical mixture of N positive and N negative charged particles in a two dimensional box of area A=L×L.A=L\times L.A=L×L. The Hamiltonian is H=∑i=12Np⃗i22m−∑i<jcicjln⁡∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣,\mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^{2N} \frac{\vec{p}_{i}^{2}}{2m} - \sum_{i<j} c_i c_j \ln |\vec{q}_i - \vec{q}_j|,H=i=1∑2N​2mp​i2​​−i<j∑​ci​cj​ln∣q​i​−q​j​∣, where ci=+c0c_i = +c_0ci​=+c0​ for i=1,…,Ni=1,\dots,Ni=1,…,N and ci=−c0c_i = -c_0ci​=−c0​ for i=N+1,…,2Ni=N+1,\dots,2Ni=N+1,…,2N. (a) In the interaction term each pair appears only once, and there is no self interaction. How many pairs have repulsive interactions, and how many have attractive interactions? (b) Write down the partition function Z(N,T,A)Z(N,T,A)Z(N,T,A) as an integral over {q⃗i},{p⃗i}\{\vec{q}_i\},\{\vec{p}_i\}{q​i​},{p​i​}. Perform the momentum integrals and rewrite the coordinate contribution as a product with powers of ∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣|\vec{q}_i - \vec{q}_j|∣q​i​−q​j​∣. (c) Although the integrals over {q⃗i}\{\vec{q}_i\}{q​i​} are not feasible exactly, the dependence of ZZZ on AAA can be obtained by rescaling q⃗i′=q⃗i/L\vec{q}_i' = \vec{q}_i/Lq​i′​=q​i​/L. Using results from (a) and (b), give the dependence Z∝A2N−βc02N/2Z \propto A^{2N - \beta c_0^{2} N/2}Z∝A2N−βc02​N/2. (d) Calculate the two-dimensional pressure PPP of this gas. (e) A hard core forbids any two particles from approaching closer than aaa. By examining ZZZ for N=1N=1N=1 (two opposite charges), estimate the temperature TcT_cTc​ at which the short-distance scale aaa becomes important and the result of (d) fails.

Extracted Answer

(a)  N(N−1) repulsive pairs,N2 attractive pairs.(b)  Z(N,T,A)=1(N!)2h4N∫∏i=12Nd2qi d2pi e−βH=1(N!)2h4N(2πmβ)2N∫A∏i=12Nd2qi ∏i<j∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣βcicj=1(N!)2h4N(2πmβ)2N∫A∏i=12Nd2qi ∏i<jci=cj∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣βc02∏i,jci=+c0, cj=−c0∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣βc02.(c)  Z∝A 2N−βc02N/2.(d)  P=kBT(∂ln⁡Z∂A)N,T=1A(2NkBT−Nc022).(e)  Tc=c022kB; for T≤Tc the hard-core scale a controls the integral and (d) fails.\begin{aligned} \text{(a)}\;& N(N-1)\ \text{repulsive pairs},\qquad N^2\ \text{attractive pairs}.\\ \text{(b)}\;& Z(N,T,A)=\frac{1}{(N!)^2h^{4N}} \int \prod_{i=1}^{2N} d^2q_i\,d^2p_i\, e^{-\beta\mathcal H}\\ &=\frac{1}{(N!)^2h^{4N}} \left(\frac{2\pi m}{\beta}\right)^{2N} \int_A \prod_{i=1}^{2N} d^2q_i\, \prod_{i<j}|\vec q_i-\vec q_j|^{\beta c_ic_j}\\ &=\frac{1}{(N!)^2h^{4N}} \left(\frac{2\pi m}{\beta}\right)^{2N} \int_A \prod_{i=1}^{2N} d^2q_i\, \frac{ \prod_{\substack{i<j\\ c_i=c_j}}|\vec q_i-\vec q_j|^{\beta c_0^2} }{ \prod_{\substack{i,j\\ c_i=+c_0,\ c_j=-c_0}}|\vec q_i-\vec q_j|^{\beta c_0^2} }.\\ \text{(c)}\;& Z\propto A^{\,2N-\beta c_0^2N/2}.\\ \text{(d)}\;& P=k_BT\left(\frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial A}\right)_{N,T} =\frac{1}{A}\left(2Nk_BT-\frac{Nc_0^2}{2}\right).\\ \text{(e)}\;& T_c=\frac{c_0^2}{2k_B};\ \text{for }T\le T_c\text{ the hard-core scale }a\text{ controls the integral and (d) fails.} \end{aligned}(a)(b)(c)(d)(e)​N(N−1) repulsive pairs,N2 attractive pairs.Z(N,T,A)=(N!)2h4N1​∫i=1∏2N​d2qi​d2pi​e−βH=(N!)2h4N1​(β2πm​)2N∫A​i=1∏2N​d2qi​i<j∏​∣q​i​−q​j​∣βci​cj​=(N!)2h4N1​(β2πm​)2N∫A​i=1∏2N​d2qi​∏i,jci​=+c0​, cj​=−c0​​​∣q​i​−q​j​∣βc02​∏i<jci​=cj​​​∣q​i​−q​j​∣βc02​​.Z∝A2N−βc02​N/2.P=kB​T(∂A∂lnZ​)N,T​=A1​(2NkB​T−2Nc02​​).Tc​=2kB​c02​​; for T≤Tc​ the hard-core scale a controls the integral and (d) fails.​

Final Solution

(a) There are NNN positive and NNN negative charges. Opposite-charge pairs: N×N=N2N\times N = N^{2}N×N=N2 (attractive). Like-charge pairs: choose 2 among NNN positives or NNN negatives: 2(N2)=N(N−1)2 \binom{N}{2} = N(N-1)2(2N​)=N(N−1) (repulsive). (b) The partition function is Z(N,T,A)=1(N!)2h4N∫∏i=12Nd2q⃗i d2p⃗i exp⁡[−β∑i=12Npi22m+β∑i<jcicjln⁡∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣].Z(N,T,A) = \frac{1}{(N!)^{2} h^{4N}} \int \prod_{i=1}^{2N} d^{2}\vec{q}_i \, d^{2}\vec{p}_i \, \exp\Big[ -\beta \sum_{i=1}^{2N} \frac{p_i^{2}}{2m} + \beta \sum_{i<j} c_i c_j \ln |\vec{q}_i - \vec{q}_j| \Big].Z(N,T,A)=(N!)2h4N1​∫i=1∏2N​d2q​i​d2p​i​exp[−βi=1∑2N​2mpi2​​+βi<j∑​ci​cj​ln∣q​i​−q​j​∣]. Gaussian momentum integrals give λ=h/2πmkBT\lambda = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}λ=h/2πmkB​T​ and Z(N,T,A)=1λ4N(N!)2∫∏i=12Nd2q⃗i∏i<j2N∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣βcicj.Z(N,T,A) = \frac{1}{\lambda^{4N} (N!)^{2}} \int \prod_{i=1}^{2N} d^{2}\vec{q}_i \prod_{i<j}^{2N} |\vec{q}_i - \vec{q}_j|^{\beta c_i c_j}.Z(N,T,A)=λ4N(N!)21​∫i=1∏2N​d2q​i​i<j∏2N​∣q​i​−q​j​∣βci​cj​. (c) With q⃗i=Lq⃗i′\vec{q}_i = L \vec{q}'_iq​i​=Lq​i′​, one has d2q⃗i=L2d2q⃗i′d^{2}\vec{q}_i = L^{2} d^{2}\vec{q}'_id2q​i​=L2d2q​i′​ and ∣q⃗i−q⃗j∣=L∣q⃗i′−q⃗j′∣|\vec{q}_i - \vec{q}_j| = L |\vec{q}'_i - \vec{q}'_j|∣q​i​−q​j​∣=L∣q​i′​−q​j′​∣. Counting powers of LLL, the measure contributes L4NL^{4N}L4N; the interactions contribute Lβc02[N(N−1)]L^{\beta c_0^2[N(N-1)]}Lβc02​[N(N−1)] from repulsions and L−βc02[N2]L^{-\beta c_0^2 [N^{2}]}L−βc02​[N2] from attractions. Hence Z(N,T,A)∝L4N−βc02N=A2N−(βc02N)/2.Z(N,T,A) \propto L^{4N - \beta c_0^{2} N} = A^{2N - (\beta c_0^{2} N)/2}.Z(N,T,A)∝L4N−βc02​N=A2N−(βc02​N)/2. (d) The pressure is P=kBT ∂ln⁡Z∂A∣N,T=kBT∂∂A[(2N−βc02N2)ln⁡A+const]=2NkBTA−Nc02kBT2A.P = k_B T \, \frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial A}\Big|_{N,T} = k_B T \frac{\partial}{\partial A}\Big[ \big(2N - \tfrac{\beta c_0^2 N}{2}\big) \ln A + \text{const} \Big] = \frac{2N k_B T}{A} - \frac{N c_0^{2} k_B T}{2A}.P=kB​T∂A∂lnZ​​N,T​=kB​T∂A∂​[(2N−2βc02​N​)lnA+const]=A2NkB​T​−2ANc02​kB​T​. At high TTT this reduces to the ideal-gas value for 2N2N2N particles; at low TTT the expression becomes negative, signaling the breakdown due to collapse. (e) For one positive and one negative charge (N=1N=1N=1), with hard core aaa and system size LLL, the coordinate integral in center-of-mass and relative variables is Z(N=1,T,A)=Aλ4∫d2q⃗ q−βc02≈2πAλ4∫aLdq q1−βc02=2πAλ4L2−βc02−a2−βc022−βc02.Z(N{=}1,T,A) = \frac{A}{\lambda^{4}} \int d^{2}\vec{q} \, q^{-\beta c_0^{2}} \approx \frac{2\pi A}{\lambda^{4}} \int_{a}^{L} dq\, q^{1-\beta c_0^{2}} = \frac{2\pi A}{\lambda^{4}} \frac{L^{2-\beta c_0^{2}} - a^{2-\beta c_0^{2}}}{2 - \beta c_0^{2}}.Z(N=1,T,A)=λ4A​∫d2q​q−βc02​≈λ42πA​∫aL​dqq1−βc02​=λ42πA​2−βc02​L2−βc02​−a2−βc02​​. If 2−βc02<02 - \beta c_0^{2} < 02−βc02​<0 (low TTT), the short-distance cutoff aaa dominates; if 2−βc02>02 - \beta c_0^{2} > 02−βc02​>0 (high TTT), the system size LLL dominates as in (c). The crossover is at βc02=2\beta c_0^{2} = 2βc02​=2, i.e. Tc=c022kB.T_c = \frac{c_0^{2}}{2 k_B}.Tc​=2kB​c02​​. For T>TcT>T_cT>Tc​ the system is a dissociated plasma; for T<TcT<T_cT<Tc​ it forms tightly bound opposite-charge dipoles (no collapse due to the hard core).

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly gives N(N-1) like-charge repulsive pairs and N^2 opposite-charge attractive pairs.
  • (b) Correctly writes the indistinguishable-particle partition function, performs the Gaussian momentum integrals, and expresses the coordinate integral as products with powers beta c_i c_j, including like-charge factors in the numerator and opposite-charge factors in the denominator.
  • (c) Correctly states the area dependence Z proportional to A^(2N - beta c0^2 N/2).
  • (d) Correctly differentiates the area dependence to obtain P = (2N k_B T - N c0^2/2)/A, equivalent to k_B T(2N - beta c0^2 N/2)/A.
  • (e) Correctly identifies the crossover beta c0^2 = 2, giving T_c = c0^2/(2 k_B), and states that below this scale the hard core controls the integral and the pressure result fails.

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

9. lattice/6.6

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

One dimensional gas: The transfer matrix method can also be applied to a one dimensional gas of particles with short-range interactions. For a potential with a hard core that screens the interactions from further neighbors, the Hamiltonian for N particles is H=∑i=1Npi22m+∑i=2NV(xi−xi−1).\mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{p_i^2}{2m} + \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1}).H=i=1∑N​2mpi2​​+i=2∑N​V(xi​−xi−1​). The (indistinguishable) particles are labeled with coordinates \{x_i\} such that 0≤x1≤x2≤⋯≤xN≤L,0 \le x_1 \le x_2 \le \dots \le x_N \le L,0≤x1​≤x2​≤⋯≤xN​≤L, where L is the length of the box confining the particles. (b) Write the expression for the partition function Z(T,N,L). Change variables to δ1=x1,δ2=x2−x1,…,δN=xN−xN−1,andcarefullyindicatetheallowedrangesofintegrationandtheconstraints.\delta_1 = x_1, \delta_2 = x_2 - x_1, \dots, \delta_N = x_N - x_{N-1}, and carefully indicate the allowed ranges of integration and the constraints.δ1​=x1​,δ2​=x2​−x1​,…,δN​=xN​−xN−1​,andcarefullyindicatetheallowedrangesofintegrationandtheconstraints. (c) Consider the Gibbs partition function obtained from the Laplace transformation Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞dLexp⁡(−βPL)Z(T,N,L),Z(T,N,P) = \int_0^\infty dL \exp(-\beta P L) Z(T,N,L),Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞​dLexp(−βPL)Z(T,N,L), and find the standard formula for P in the canonical ensemble. (d) Change variables from L to δN+1=L−∑i=1Nδi,\delta_{N+1} = L - \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i,δN+1​=L−∑i=1N​δi​, and find the expression for Z(T,N,P) as a product over one-dimensional integrals over each δi.\delta_i.δi​. (e) At a fixed pressure P, find expressions for the mean length L(T,N,P), and the density n = N/L(T,N,P) (involving ratios of integrals). (f) For a hard sphere gas, with minimum separation a between particles, calculate the equation of state P(T,n).

Extracted Answer

(b)Z(T,N,L)=λT−N∫0∞∏i=1Ndδi Θ ⁣(L−∑i=1Nδi)exp⁡ ⁣[−β∑i=2NV(δi)],δ1=x1,δi=xi−xi−1 (2≤i≤N),δi≥0,∑i=1Nδi≤L.(c)Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞dL e−βPLZ(T,N,L),P=kBT(∂ln⁡Z(T,N,L)∂L)T,N.(d)δN+1=L−∑i=1Nδi≥0,Z(T,N,P)=λT−N(∫0∞dδ e−βPδ)2(∫0∞dδ e−β[Pδ+V(δ)])N−1.(e)L(T,N,P)=2βP+(N−1)∫0∞dδ δ e−β[Pδ+V(δ)]∫0∞dδ e−β[Pδ+V(δ)],n=NL(T,N,P).(f)V(δ)=∞ for δ<a,V(δ)=0 for δ≥a,L=2βP+(N−1)(a+1βP),and in the thermodynamic limitP=nkBT1−na.\begin{aligned} \text{(b)}\quad &Z(T,N,L)=\lambda_T^{-N} \int_0^\infty \prod_{i=1}^N d\delta_i\, \Theta\!\left(L-\sum_{i=1}^N\delta_i\right) \exp\!\left[-\beta\sum_{i=2}^N\mathcal V(\delta_i)\right], \\ &\delta_1=x_1,\quad \delta_i=x_i-x_{i-1}\ (2\le i\le N),\quad \delta_i\ge0,\quad \sum_{i=1}^N\delta_i\le L. \\[2mm] \text{(c)}\quad &Z(T,N,P)=\int_0^\infty dL\,e^{-\beta PL}Z(T,N,L), \qquad P=k_BT\left(\frac{\partial \ln Z(T,N,L)}{\partial L}\right)_{T,N}. \\[2mm] \text{(d)}\quad &\delta_{N+1}=L-\sum_{i=1}^N\delta_i\ge0,\qquad Z(T,N,P)=\lambda_T^{-N} \left(\int_0^\infty d\delta\,e^{-\beta P\delta}\right)^2 \left(\int_0^\infty d\delta\,e^{-\beta[P\delta+\mathcal V(\delta)]}\right)^{N-1}. \\[2mm] \text{(e)}\quad &L(T,N,P)=\frac{2}{\beta P} +(N-1) \frac{\int_0^\infty d\delta\,\delta\,e^{-\beta[P\delta+\mathcal V(\delta)]}} {\int_0^\infty d\delta\,e^{-\beta[P\delta+\mathcal V(\delta)]}}, \qquad n=\frac{N}{L(T,N,P)}. \\[2mm] \text{(f)}\quad &\mathcal V(\delta)=\infty\ \text{for }\delta<a,\quad \mathcal V(\delta)=0\ \text{for }\delta\ge a, \\ &L=\frac{2}{\beta P}+(N-1)\left(a+\frac{1}{\beta P}\right), \quad \text{and in the thermodynamic limit}\quad P=\frac{n k_BT}{1-na}. \end{aligned}(b)(c)(d)(e)(f)​Z(T,N,L)=λT−N​∫0∞​i=1∏N​dδi​Θ(L−i=1∑N​δi​)exp[−βi=2∑N​V(δi​)],δ1​=x1​,δi​=xi​−xi−1​ (2≤i≤N),δi​≥0,i=1∑N​δi​≤L.Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞​dLe−βPLZ(T,N,L),P=kB​T(∂L∂lnZ(T,N,L)​)T,N​.δN+1​=L−i=1∑N​δi​≥0,Z(T,N,P)=λT−N​(∫0∞​dδe−βPδ)2(∫0∞​dδe−β[Pδ+V(δ)])N−1.L(T,N,P)=βP2​+(N−1)∫0∞​dδe−β[Pδ+V(δ)]∫0∞​dδδe−β[Pδ+V(δ)]​,n=L(T,N,P)N​.V(δ)=∞ for δ<a,V(δ)=0 for δ≥a,L=βP2​+(N−1)(a+βP1​),and in the thermodynamic limitP=1−nankB​T​.​

Final Solution

(a) Hard cores screen further-neighbor interactions, leaving only nearest-neighbor potentials. Including kinetic energies, H=∑i=1Npi22m+∑i=2NV(xi−xi−1),0≤x1≤x2≤⋯≤xN≤L.\mathcal{H} = \sum_{i=1}^N \frac{p_i^2}{2m} + \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1}), \quad 0 \le x_1 \le x_2 \le \cdots \le x_N \le L.H=i=1∑N​2mpi2​​+i=2∑N​V(xi​−xi−1​),0≤x1​≤x2​≤⋯≤xN​≤L. (b) The canonical partition function is Z(T,N,L)=1hN∫0Ldx1∫x1Ldx2⋯∫xN−1LdxN e−β∑i=2NV(xi−xi−1)∏i=1N∫−∞∞dpi e−βpi2/(2m).Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{h^N} \int_0^L dx_1 \int_{x_1}^L dx_2 \cdots \int_{x_{N-1}}^L dx_N\, e^{-\beta \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1})} \prod_{i=1}^N \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} dp_i\, e^{-\beta p_i^2/(2m)}.Z(T,N,L)=hN1​∫0L​dx1​∫x1​L​dx2​⋯∫xN−1​L​dxN​e−β∑i=2N​V(xi​−xi−1​)i=1∏N​∫−∞∞​dpi​e−βpi2​/(2m). After Gaussian momentum integrals with λ=h/2πmkBT,\lambda = h/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T},λ=h/2πmkB​T​, Z(T,N,L)=1λN∫0Ldx1∫x1Ldx2⋯∫xN−1LdxN e−β∑i=2NV(xi−xi−1).Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^L dx_1 \int_{x_1}^L dx_2 \cdots \int_{x_{N-1}}^L dx_N\, e^{-\beta \sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(x_i-x_{i-1})}.Z(T,N,L)=λN1​∫0L​dx1​∫x1​L​dx2​⋯∫xN−1​L​dxN​e−β∑i=2N​V(xi​−xi−1​). Change variables δ1=x1,δi=xi−xi−1(i≥2)sothatxN=∑i=1Nδi.\delta_1=x_1, \delta_{i}=x_i-x_{i-1} (i\ge 2) so that x_N=\sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i.δ1​=x1​,δi​=xi​−xi−1​(i≥2)sothatxN​=∑i=1N​δi​. Then Z(T,N,L)=1λN∫0Ldδ1∫0L−δ1dδ2⋯∫0L−∑i=1N−1δidδN e−β∑i=2NV(δi).Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^L d\delta_1 \int_0^{L-\delta_1} d\delta_2 \cdots \int_0^{L-\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\delta_i} d\delta_N\, e^{-\beta\sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(\delta_i)}.Z(T,N,L)=λN1​∫0L​dδ1​∫0L−δ1​​dδ2​⋯∫0L−∑i=1N−1​δi​​dδN​e−β∑i=2N​V(δi​). Equivalently, Z(T,N,L)=1λN∫0∞∏i=1Ndδi e−β∑i=2NV(δi) Θ ⁣(L−∑i=1Nδi).Z(T,N,L) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \int_0^\infty \prod_{i=1}^N d\delta_i\, e^{-\beta\sum_{i=2}^N \mathcal{V}(\delta_i)}\, \Theta\!\left(L-\sum_{i=1}^N\delta_i\right).Z(T,N,L)=λN1​∫0∞​i=1∏N​dδi​e−β∑i=2N​V(δi​)Θ(L−i=1∑N​δi​). (c) The Gibbs partition function is Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞dL e−βPLZ(T,N,L).Z(T,N,P) = \int_0^\infty dL\, e^{-\beta PL} Z(T,N,L).Z(T,N,P)=∫0∞​dLe−βPLZ(T,N,L). Extremizing the integrand with respect to L gives the canonical pressure relation P=kBT ∂ln⁡Z∂L∣T,N,P = k_B T\, \left.\frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial L}\right|_{T,N},P=kB​T∂L∂lnZ​​T,N​, consistent with F = -k_B Tln⁡ZandP=−∂F/∂L∣T,N.T\ln Z and P = -\left.\partial F/\partial L\right|_{T,N}.TlnZandP=−∂F/∂L∣T,N​. (d) Performing the Laplace transform using the step-function representation yields Z(T,N,P)=1λN(βP)2{∫0∞dδ e−β[V(δ)+Pδ]}N−1.Z(T,N,P) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N (\beta P)^2} \left\{ \int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]} \right\}^{N-1}.Z(T,N,P)=λN(βP)21​{∫0∞​dδe−β[V(δ)+Pδ]}N−1. An equivalent derivation introduces δN+1=L−∑i=1Nδiandintegrateseachδindependentlyfrom0to∞.\delta_{N+1} = L - \sum_{i=1}^N \delta_i and integrates each \delta independently from 0 to \infty.δN+1​=L−∑i=1N​δi​andintegrateseachδindependentlyfrom0to∞. (e) The mean length is L(T,N,P)=−kBT ∂∂Pln⁡Z(T,N,P)∣T,N=2βP+(N−1)∫0∞dδ δ e−β[V(δ)+Pδ]∫0∞dδ e−β[V(δ)+Pδ].L(T,N,P) = -k_B T\, \frac{\partial}{\partial P} \ln Z(T,N,P)\bigg|_{T,N} = \frac{2}{\beta P} + (N-1) \frac{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, \delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}.L(T,N,P)=−kB​T∂P∂​lnZ(T,N,P)​T,N​=βP2​+(N−1)∫0∞​dδe−β[V(δ)+Pδ]∫0∞​dδδe−β[V(δ)+Pδ]​. Thus n=NL(T,N,P)=N{2kBTP+(N−1)∫0∞dδ δ e−β[V(δ)+Pδ]∫0∞dδ e−β[V(δ)+Pδ]}−1.n = \frac{N}{L(T,N,P)} = N \left\{ \frac{2k_B T}{P} + (N-1) \frac{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, \delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}}{\int_0^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta[\mathcal{V}(\delta)+P\delta]}} \right\}^{-1}.n=L(T,N,P)N​=N{P2kB​T​+(N−1)∫0∞​dδe−β[V(δ)+Pδ]∫0∞​dδδe−β[V(δ)+Pδ]​}−1. For an ideal gas (V=0),Li.g.(T,N,P)=(N+1)kBT/P,andni.g.=NN+1 PkBT.\mathcal{V}=0), L_{\text{i.g.}}(T,N,P) = (N+1)k_B T/P, and n_{\text{i.g.}} = \frac{N}{N+1}\, \frac{P}{k_B T}.V=0),Li.g.​(T,N,P)=(N+1)kB​T/P,andni.g.​=N+1N​kB​TP​. (f) Hard spheres: impose δi≥afori=2,…,N,so\delta_i \ge a for i=2,\dots,N, soδi​≥afori=2,…,N,so Z(T,N,P)=1λN(βP)2[∫a∞dδ e−βPδ]N−1=1λN(1βP)N+1e−βPa(N−1).Z(T,N,P) = \frac{1}{\lambda^N (\beta P)^2} \left[ \int_a^\infty d\delta\, e^{-\beta P\delta} \right]^{N-1} = \frac{1}{\lambda^N} \left( \frac{1}{\beta P} \right)^{N+1} e^{-\beta P a (N-1)}.Z(T,N,P)=λN(βP)21​[∫a∞​dδe−βPδ]N−1=λN1​(βP1​)N+1e−βPa(N−1). Then L=−kBT∂ln⁡Z∂P∣T,N=(N+1)kBTP+(N−1)a.L = -k_B T \frac{\partial \ln Z}{\partial P}\bigg|_{T,N} = \frac{(N+1)k_B T}{P} + (N-1)a.L=−kB​T∂P∂lnZ​​T,N​=P(N+1)kB​T​+(N−1)a. Rearranging, βP=N+1L−(N−1)a=n+1/L1−(n−1/L)a≈n (1+na+n2a2+⋯ ),\beta P = \frac{N+1}{L-(N-1)a} = \frac{n+1/L}{1-(n-1/L)a} \approx n\,(1+na+n^2a^2+\cdots),βP=L−(N−1)aN+1​=1−(n−1/L)an+1/L​≈n(1+na+n2a2+⋯), so the virial series is βP≈n+an2+a2n3+⋯withBℓ(T)=aℓ−1.\beta P \approx n + a n^2 + a^2 n^3 + \cdots with B_\ell(T) = a^{\ell-1}.βP≈n+an2+a2n3+⋯withBℓ​(T)=aℓ−1.

Judge Rationale

  • (b) Correctly gives the canonical partition function after momentum integration, changes to the delta variables, and states the nonnegative ranges with the constraint sum_i delta_i <= L.
  • (c) Correctly states the Laplace-transformed Gibbs partition function and the canonical pressure relation P = k_B T (partial ln Z / partial L)_{T,N}.
  • (d) Correctly introduces delta_{N+1} and factorizes the Gibbs partition function into two free-gap integrals and N-1 interacting nearest-neighbor integrals, equivalent to the reference expression.
  • (e) Correctly gives the mean length as 2/(beta P) plus (N-1) times the ratio of first-moment and zeroth-moment interaction integrals, and states n = N/L.
  • (f) Correctly implements the hard-core lower limit, obtains L = (N+1)/(beta P) + (N-1)a, and gives the thermodynamic-limit equation of state P = n k_B T/(1-na).

FrontierPhysics · test · merged mode

10. textbook/7.13

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Freezing of He^4: At low temperatures He^4 can be converted from liquid to solid by application of pressure. An interesting feature of the phase boundary is that the melting pressure is reduced slightly from its T=0 T = 0\,T=0K value, by approximately 20 N m−220\,\text{N m}^{-2}20N m−2 at its minimum at T=0.8 T = 0.8\,T=0.8K. We will use a simple model of liquid and solid phases of He^4 to account for this feature. (a) The important excitations in liquid He^4 at T<1T<1T<1 K are phonons of velocity ccc. Calculate the contribution of these modes to the heat capacity per particle CVℓ/NC_V^{\ell}/NCVℓ​/N, of the liquid. (b) Calculate the low temperature heat capacity per particle CVs/NC_V^s/NCVs​/N, of solid He^4 in terms of longitudinal and transverse sound velocities cLc_LcL​, and cTc_TcT​. (c) Using the above results calculate the entropy difference (sℓ−ss)(s_{\ell} - s_s)(sℓ​−ss​), assuming a single sound velocity c≈cL≈cTc \approx c_L \approx c_Tc≈cL​≈cT​, and approximately equal volumes per particle vℓ≈vs≈vv_{\ell} \approx v_s \approx vvℓ​≈vs​≈v. Which phase (solid or liquid) has the higher entropy? (d) Assuming a small (temperature independent) volume difference δv=vℓ−vs\delta v = v_{\ell} - v_{s}δv=vℓ​−vs​, calculate the form of the melting curve. To explain the anomaly described at the beginning, which phase (solid or liquid) must have the higher density?

Extracted Answer

(a)CVℓN=2π215 vℓ kB4T3ℏ3c3=2π215 kB vℓ(kBTℏc)3.(b)CVsN=2π215 vs kB4T3ℏ3(1cL3+2cT3).(c)sℓ−ss=2π245 v kB4T3ℏ3(1c3−3c3)=−4π245 v kB4T3ℏ3c3,so the solid has higher entropy.(d)dPmdT=sℓ−ssδv=−4π245 vδv kB4T3ℏ3c3,Pm(T)−Pm(0)=−π245 vδv kB4T4ℏ3c3. For the melting pressure to decrease with T, δv>0, so vℓ>vs; the solid must have the higher density.\begin{aligned} \text{(a)}\quad &\frac{C_V^\ell}{N} = \frac{2\pi^2}{15}\,v_\ell\,\frac{k_B^4T^3}{\hbar^3c^3} = \frac{2\pi^2}{15}\,k_B\,v_\ell\left(\frac{k_BT}{\hbar c}\right)^3.\\[3pt] \text{(b)}\quad &\frac{C_V^s}{N} = \frac{2\pi^2}{15}\,v_s\,\frac{k_B^4T^3}{\hbar^3} \left(\frac{1}{c_L^3}+\frac{2}{c_T^3}\right).\\[3pt] \text{(c)}\quad & s_\ell-s_s = \frac{2\pi^2}{45}\,v\,\frac{k_B^4T^3}{\hbar^3} \left(\frac{1}{c^3}-\frac{3}{c^3}\right) = -\frac{4\pi^2}{45}\,v\,\frac{k_B^4T^3}{\hbar^3c^3}, \quad \text{so the solid has higher entropy.}\\[3pt] \text{(d)}\quad & \frac{dP_m}{dT} = \frac{s_\ell-s_s}{\delta v} = -\frac{4\pi^2}{45}\,\frac{v}{\delta v}\, \frac{k_B^4T^3}{\hbar^3c^3}, \\ & P_m(T)-P_m(0) = -\frac{\pi^2}{45}\,\frac{v}{\delta v}\, \frac{k_B^4T^4}{\hbar^3c^3}. \ \text{For the melting pressure to decrease with }T,\ \delta v>0,\text{ so }v_\ell>v_s; \text{ the solid must have the higher density.} \end{aligned}(a)(b)(c)(d)​NCVℓ​​=152π2​vℓ​ℏ3c3kB4​T3​=152π2​kB​vℓ​(ℏckB​T​)3.NCVs​​=152π2​vs​ℏ3kB4​T3​(cL3​1​+cT3​2​).sℓ​−ss​=452π2​vℏ3kB4​T3​(c31​−c33​)=−454π2​vℏ3c3kB4​T3​,so the solid has higher entropy.dTdPm​​=δvsℓ​−ss​​=−454π2​δvv​ℏ3c3kB4​T3​,Pm​(T)−Pm​(0)=−45π2​δvv​ℏ3c3kB4​T4​. For the melting pressure to decrease with T, δv>0, so vℓ​>vs​; the solid must have the higher density.​

Final Solution

(a) For phonons with ε=ℏck\varepsilon=\hbar c kε=ℏck, Eph=∑k⃗ℏck/(eβℏck−1)=V2π2ℏc(kBTℏc)4∫0∞dxx3ex−1=π230Vℏc(kBT/ℏc)4E_{\text{ph}} = \sum_{\vec{k}} \hbar c k/(e^{\beta \hbar c k}-1) = \dfrac{V}{2\pi^2} \hbar c \left(\dfrac{k_B T}{\hbar c}\right)^4 \int_0^{\infty} dx \dfrac{x^3}{e^x-1} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{30} V \hbar c (k_B T/\hbar c)^4Eph​=∑k​ℏck/(eβℏck−1)=2π2V​ℏc(ℏckB​T​)4∫0∞​dxex−1x3​=30π2​Vℏc(kB​T/ℏc)4. Hence CV=dE/dT=2π215VkB(kBT/ℏc)3C_V = dE/dT = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{15} V k_B (k_B T/\hbar c)^3CV​=dE/dT=152π2​VkB​(kB​T/ℏc)3, so CVℓ/N=2π215kBvℓ(kBT/ℏc)3C_V^{\ell}/N = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{15} k_B v_{\ell} (k_B T/\hbar c)^3CVℓ​/N=152π2​kB​vℓ​(kB​T/ℏc)3. (b) In the solid there are two transverse modes (cTc_TcT​) and one longitudinal (cLc_LcL​), yielding CVs/N=2π215kBvs(kBT/ℏ)3(2/cT3+1/cL3)C_V^s/N = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{15} k_B v_s (k_B T/\hbar)^3 (2/c_T^3 + 1/c_L^3)CVs​/N=152π2​kB​vs​(kB​T/ℏ)3(2/cT3​+1/cL3​). (c) The entropies are s=∫0TCV(T′) dT′/T′s = \int_0^T C_V(T')\, dT'/T's=∫0T​CV​(T′)dT′/T′. Thus sℓ=2π245kBvℓ(kBT/ℏc)3s_{\ell} = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{45} k_B v_{\ell} (k_B T/\hbar c)^3sℓ​=452π2​kB​vℓ​(kB​T/ℏc)3,   ss=2π245kBvs(kBT/ℏ)3(2/cT3+1/cL3)\; s_s = \dfrac{2\pi^2}{45} k_B v_s (k_B T/\hbar)^3 (2/c_T^3 + 1/c_L^3)ss​=452π2​kB​vs​(kB​T/ℏ)3(2/cT3​+1/cL3​). For cL≈cT≈cc_L\approx c_T\approx ccL​≈cT​≈c and vℓ≈vs≈vv_{\ell}\approx v_s\approx vvℓ​≈vs​≈v, sℓ−ss≈−4π245kBv(kBT/ℏc)3s_{\ell}-s_s \approx -\dfrac{4\pi^2}{45} k_B v (k_B T/\hbar c)^3sℓ​−ss​≈−454π2​kB​v(kB​T/ℏc)3. The solid has higher entropy (three phonon branches vs one). (d) Clausius-Clapeyron gives dP/dT=(sℓ−ss)/(vℓ−vs)dP/dT = (s_{\ell}-s_s)/(v_{\ell}-v_s)dP/dT=(sℓ​−ss​)/(vℓ​−vs​). With δv=vℓ−vs>0\delta v = v_{\ell}-v_s>0δv=vℓ​−vs​>0 to explain decreasing melting pressure, one obtains dPdT=−4π245kBvδv(kBT/ℏc)3\dfrac{dP}{dT} = -\dfrac{4\pi^2}{45} k_B \dfrac{v}{\delta v} (k_B T/\hbar c)^3dTdP​=−454π2​kB​δvv​(kB​T/ℏc)3, integrating to Pmelt(T)=P(0)−π245kBvδv(kBT/ℏc)3TP_{\text{melt}}(T) = P(0) - \dfrac{\pi^2}{45} k_B \dfrac{v}{\delta v} (k_B T/\hbar c)^3 TPmelt​(T)=P(0)−45π2​kB​δvv​(kB​T/ℏc)3T. Thus the solid must have higher density.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct heat capacity per particle for one phonon branch in the liquid, equivalent to the reference expression.
  • (b) Correct solid heat capacity per particle with one longitudinal and two transverse phonon branches.
  • (c) Correct entropy difference under the equal-velocity and equal-volume assumptions, and correctly identifies the solid as having higher entropy.
  • (d) Correct Clausius-Clapeyron form and integrated melting curve, and correctly states that decreasing melting pressure requires delta v > 0, so the solid has higher density.

Physics · test · merged mode

11. optics/2-58

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

﻿ A laser beam (λ=6.3×10−5 cm\lambda = 6.3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}λ=6.3×10−5cm) is directed at grazing incidence onto a steel machinist's ruler (which is calibrated in 1/161/161/16 inch). The light reflects from the surface of the ruler and is projected onto a vertical wall 10 meters away (Fig. 2.61). (a) Derive the condition on θ′\theta'θ′ for interference maxima on the wall. Assume for simplicity that the laser beam is initially almost parallel to the ruler surface. (b) What is the vertical separation on the wall of the zeroth and first order spots of the pattern?

Extracted Answer

(a) d(1−cos⁡θ′)=mλ,d=116 in, m=0,1,2,…;(b) y1−y0=Ltan⁡θ1′≈0.28 m =28 cm.\text{(a) }d(1-\cos\theta')=m\lambda,\quad d=\frac{1}{16}\text{ in},\ m=0,1,2,\ldots;\qquad \text{(b) }y_1-y_0=L\tan\theta'_1\approx 0.28\text{ m }=28\text{ cm}.(a) d(1−cosθ′)=mλ,d=161​ in, m=0,1,2,…;(b) y1​−y0​=Ltanθ1′​≈0.28 m =28 cm.

Final Solution

﻿ - (a) The optical path length difference (OPD) of the parallel light rays shown in Fig. 2.61 is Δ=d(cos⁡θ−cos⁡θ′)≈d(1−cos⁡θ′)\Delta = d(\cos \theta - \cos \theta') \approx d(1 - \cos \theta')Δ=d(cosθ−cosθ′)≈d(1−cosθ′) as θ≈0\theta \approx 0θ≈0. Interference maxima occur when Δ=mλ\Delta = m\lambdaΔ=mλ (m is an integer)(m \text{ is an integer})(m is an integer), or cos⁡θ′=1−mλ/d.\cos \theta' = 1 - m\lambda / d.cosθ′=1−mλ/d. - (b) When m=0m = 0m=0, θ′=0\theta' = 0θ′=0. When m=1m = 1m=1, θ1′=1−λ/d=0.9996\theta'_1 = 1 - \lambda / d = 0.9996θ1′​=1−λ/d=0.9996 taking d=1d = 1d=1 division (116 inch)\left(\frac{1}{16} \text{ inch}\right)(161​ inch). The vertical separation on the wall between the zeroth and first order spots is x=l⋅tan⁡θ1′=0.28 m.x = l \cdot \tan \theta'_1 = 0.28 \text{ m}.x=l⋅tanθ1′​=0.28 m.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly states the interference maximum condition for grazing incidence as d(1-cos theta') = m lambda, equivalent to cos theta' = 1 - m lambda/d.
  • (b) Correctly gives the wall separation as L tan(theta'_1) and the numerical value approximately 0.28 m (28 cm), consistent with the reference solution.

Physics · test · merged mode

12. electro/1_17

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

The inside of a grounded spherical metal shell (inner radius R1R_1R1​ and outer radius R2R_2R2​) is filled with space charge of uniform charge density ρ\rhoρ. (a) Find the electrostatic energy of the system. (b) Find the potential at the center.

Extracted Answer

(a) U=2πρ2R1545ε0,(b) V(0)=ρR126ε0, for space charge filling the cavity r<R1.\text{(a) }U=\frac{2\pi\rho^2R_1^5}{45\varepsilon_0},\qquad \text{(b) }V(0)=\frac{\rho R_1^2}{6\varepsilon_0}\text{, for space charge filling the cavity }r<R_1.(a) U=45ε0​2πρ2R15​​,(b) V(0)=6ε0​ρR12​​, for space charge filling the cavity r<R1​.

Final Solution

### Solution: Consider a concentric spherical surface of radius r(r<R1)r(r < R_1)r(r<R1​). Using Gauss' law we get E=rρ3ε0er.\mathbf{E} = \frac{r\rho}{3\varepsilon_0} \mathbf{e}_r.E=3ε0​rρ​er​. As the shell is grounded, φ(R1)=0,E=0(r>R2)\varphi(R_1) = 0, E = 0 (r > R_2)φ(R1​)=0,E=0(r>R2​). Thus φ(r)=∫rR1Edr=ρ6ϵ0(R12−r2).\varphi(r) = \int_r^{R_1} E dr = \frac{\rho}{6 \epsilon_0} (R_1^2 - r^2).φ(r)=∫rR1​​Edr=6ϵ0​ρ​(R12​−r2). The potential at the center is φ(0)=16ϵ0ρR12.\varphi(0) = \frac{1}{6 \epsilon_0} \rho R_1^2.φ(0)=6ϵ0​1​ρR12​. The electrostatic energy is W=∫12ρφdV=12∫0R1ρ6ϵ0(R12−r2)⋅ρ⋅4πr2dr=2ρ2R1545ϵ0.W = \int \frac{1}{2} \rho \varphi dV = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{R_1} \frac{\rho}{6 \epsilon_0} (R_1^2 - r^2) \cdot \rho \cdot 4 \pi r^2 dr = \frac{2 \rho^2 R_1^5}{45 \epsilon_0}.W=∫21​ρφdV=21​∫0R1​​6ϵ0​ρ​(R12​−r2)⋅ρ⋅4πr2dr=45ϵ0​2ρ2R15​​.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct electrostatic energy for the uniformly charged cavity of the grounded shell, matching the evaluated energy integral.
  • (b) Correct center potential, V(0)=rho R_1^2/(6 epsilon_0).

Physics · test · merged mode

13. electro/1_72

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Take a very long cylinder of radius rrr made of insulating material. Spray a cloud of electrons on the surface. They are free to move about the surface so that, at first, they spread out evenly with a charge per unit area σ0\sigma_0σ0​. Then put the cylinder in a uniform applied electric field perpendicular to the axis of the cylinder. You are asked to think about the charge density on the surface of the cylinder, σ(θ)\sigma(\theta)σ(θ), as a function of the applied electric field EaE_aEa​. In doing this you may neglect the electric polarizability of the insulating cylinder. (b) Calculate the solution for σ(θ)\sigma(\theta)σ(θ) in the case of a conducting cylinder and state the range of value of EaE_aEa​ for which this solution is applicable to the case described here.

Extracted Answer

(b) σ(θ)=σ0+2ϵ0Eacos⁡θ, θ measured from the applied-field direction. This applies to the electron-coated insulating cylinder only while no positive surface charge is required: if σ0<0 is the signed initial electron charge density, then ∣Ea∣≤∣σ0∣2ϵ0. Equivalently, if σ0 denotes the positive magnitude of the electron charge density, σ(θ)=−σ0+2ϵ0Eacos⁡θ and 0≤Ea≤σ02ϵ0.\text{(b) } \sigma(\theta)=\sigma_0+2\epsilon_0 E_a\cos\theta,\ \theta\text{ measured from the applied-field direction. This applies to the electron-coated insulating cylinder only while no positive surface charge is required: if }\sigma_0<0\text{ is the signed initial electron charge density, then }|E_a|\le \frac{|\sigma_0|}{2\epsilon_0}\text{. Equivalently, if }\sigma_0\text{ denotes the positive magnitude of the electron charge density, } \sigma(\theta)=-\sigma_0+2\epsilon_0E_a\cos\theta\text{ and }0\le E_a\le \frac{\sigma_0}{2\epsilon_0}\text{.}(b) σ(θ)=σ0​+2ϵ0​Ea​cosθ, θ measured from the applied-field direction. This applies to the electron-coated insulating cylinder only while no positive surface charge is required: if σ0​<0 is the signed initial electron charge density, then ∣Ea​∣≤2ϵ0​∣σ0​∣​. Equivalently, if σ0​ denotes the positive magnitude of the electron charge density, σ(θ)=−σ0​+2ϵ0​Ea​cosθ and 0≤Ea​≤2ϵ0​σ0​​.

Final Solution

### Solution: Use cylindrical coordinates (ρ,θ,z)(\rho, \theta, z)(ρ,θ,z) with the zzz-axis along the cylindrical axis and the direction of the applied field given by θ=0\theta = 0θ=0. Let the potentials inside and outside the cylinder be φI\varphi IφI and φII\varphi IIφII respectively. As a long cylinder is assumed, φI\varphi IφI and φII\varphi IIφII are independent of zzz. As there is no charge inside and outside the cylinder, Laplace's equation applies: ∇2φI=∇2φII=0\nabla^2\varphi_I = \nabla^2 \varphi_{II} = 0∇2φI​=∇2φII​=0. The boundary conditions are φI∣ρ=r=φII∣ρ=r,(∂φI∂θ)∣ρ=r=(∂φII∂θ)∣ρ=r,\varphi_I \big|_{\rho=r} = \varphi_{II} \big|_{\rho=r}, \quad \left( \frac{\partial \varphi_I}{\partial \theta} \right) \bigg|_{\rho=r} = \left( \frac{\partial \varphi_{II}}{\partial \theta} \right) \bigg|_{\rho=r},φI​​ρ=r​=φII​​ρ=r​,(∂θ∂φI​​)​ρ=r​=(∂θ∂φII​​)​ρ=r​, φI∣ρ→0 is finite,φII∣ρ→∞→−Eaρcos⁡θ.\varphi_I \big|_{\rho \to 0} \text{ is finite}, \\ \varphi_{II} \big|_{\rho \to \infty} \rightarrow - E_a \rho \cos \theta.φI​​ρ→0​ is finite,φII​​ρ→∞​→−Ea​ρcosθ. Note the first two conditions arise from the continuity of the tangential component of the electric intensity vector. Furthermore as the electrons are free to move about the cylindrical surface, E0=0E_0 = 0E0​=0 on the surface at equilibrium. As zzz is not involved, try solutions of the form φI=A1+B1ln⁡ρ+C1ρcos⁡θ+D1ρcos⁡θ,\varphi_I = A_1 + B_1 \ln \rho + C_1 \rho \cos \theta + \frac{D_1}{\rho} \cos \theta,φI​=A1​+B1​lnρ+C1​ρcosθ+ρD1​​cosθ, φII=A2+B2ln⁡ρ+C2ρcos⁡θ+D2ρcos⁡θ.\varphi_{II} = A_2 + B_2 \ln \rho + C_2 \rho \cos \theta + \frac{D_2}{\rho} \cos \theta.φII​=A2​+B2​lnρ+C2​ρcosθ+ρD2​​cosθ. Then the above boundary conditions require that B1=D1=0,C2=−Ea,B_1 = D_1 = 0, \quad C_2 = -E_a,B1​=D1​=0,C2​=−Ea​, and A1+C1rcos⁡θ=A2+B2ln⁡r−Earcos⁡θ+D2rcos⁡θ,A_1 + C_1 r \cos \theta = A_2 + B_2 \ln r - E_a r \cos \theta + \frac{D_2}{r} \cos \theta,A1​+C1​rcosθ=A2​+B2​lnr−Ea​rcosθ+rD2​​cosθ, −C1sin⁡θ=Easin⁡θ−D2r2sin⁡θ=0.- C_1 \sin \theta = E_a \sin \theta - \frac{D_2}{r^2} \sin \theta = 0.−C1​sinθ=Ea​sinθ−r2D2​​sinθ=0. The last equation gives C1=0,D2=Ear2.C_1 = 0, \quad D_2 = E_a r^2.C1​=0,D2​=Ea​r2. Applying Gauss' law to unit length of the cylinder: ∮EII⋅dS=qε0,\oint \mathbf{E_{II}} \cdot d\mathbf{S} = \frac{q}{\varepsilon_0},∮EII​⋅dS=ε0​q​, i.e., −B2r⋅2πr=1ε0σ0⋅2πr,-\frac{B_2}{r} \cdot 2 \pi r = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0} \sigma_0 \cdot 2 \pi r,−rB2​​⋅2πr=ε0​1​σ0​⋅2πr, we obtain B2=−σ0rε0.B_2 = -\frac{\sigma_0 r}{\varepsilon_0}.B2​=−ε0​σ0​r​. Neglecting any possible constant potential, we take A2=0A_2 = 0A2​=0. Then A1=B2ln⁡r=−σ0aln⁡rε0.A_1 = B_2 \ln r = -\frac{\sigma_0 a \ln r}{\varepsilon_0}.A1​=B2​lnr=−ε0​σ0​alnr​. We ultimately obtain the following expressions φI=−σ0rln⁡rε0,EI=0,\varphi_I = -\frac{\sigma_0 r \ln r}{\varepsilon_0}, \quad E_I = 0,φI​=−ε0​σ0​rlnr​,EI​=0, φII=−σ0rln⁡ρε0−Eaρcos⁡θ+Ear2ρcos⁡θ,\varphi_{II} = -\frac{\sigma_0 r \ln \rho}{\varepsilon_0} - E_a \rho \cos \theta + \frac{E_a r^2}{\rho} \cos \theta,φII​=−ε0​σ0​rlnρ​−Ea​ρcosθ+ρEa​r2​cosθ, EII=(σrε0ρ+Eacos⁡θ+Ear2ρ2cos⁡θ)eρ\mathbf{E}_{II} = \left( \frac{\sigma r}{\varepsilon_0 \rho} + E_a \cos \theta + \frac{E_a r^2}{\rho^2} \cos \theta \right) \mathbf{e}_\rhoEII​=(ε0​ρσr​+Ea​cosθ+ρ2Ea​r2​cosθ)eρ​ −Ea(1−r2ρ2)sin⁡θeθ,- E_a \left(1 - \frac{r^2}{\rho^2} \right) \sin \theta e_\theta,−Ea​(1−ρ2r2​)sinθeθ​, ρ(θ)=DII,∣ρ=r=σ0+2ε0Eacos⁡θ.\rho(\theta) = D_{II}, |_{\rho=r} = \sigma_0 + 2\varepsilon_0 E_a \cos \theta.ρ(θ)=DII​,∣ρ=r​=σ0​+2ε0​Ea​cosθ. (a) The difference between this case and the case of the cylindrical conductor lies in the fact that σ(θ)\sigma(\theta)σ(θ) can be positive or negative for a conductor, while in this case σ(θ)≤0\sigma(\theta) \leq 0σ(θ)≤0. However, when ∣Ea∣<∣σ02ε0∣|E_a| < \left|\frac{\sigma_0}{2\varepsilon_0}\right|∣Ea​∣<​2ε0​σ0​​​, the two problems have the same solution. (b) For the case of a conducting cylinder the electrostatic field must satisfy the following: (1) Inside the conductor E1=0E_1 = 0E1​=0 and φ1\varphi_1φ1​ is a constant. (2) Outside the conductor ∇2φII=0,\nabla^2 \varphi_{II} = 0,∇2φII​=0, (∂φII∂θ)ρ=r=0,φII∣ρ→∞→−Eaρcos⁡θ.\left( \frac{\partial \varphi_{II}}{\partial \theta} \right)_{\rho=r} = 0, \quad \varphi_{II} |_{\rho \rightarrow \infty} \rightarrow -E_a \rho \cos \theta.(∂θ∂φII​​)ρ=r​=0,φII​∣ρ→∞​→−Ea​ρcosθ. The solution for φII\varphi_{II}φII​ is the same as before. For the solution of the conductor to fit the case of an insulating cylinder, the necessary condition is ∣Ea∣≤∣σ02ε0∣|E_a| \leq \left|\frac{\sigma_0}{2\varepsilon_0}\right|∣Ea​∣≤​2ε0​σ0​​​, which ensures that the surface charge density on the cylinder is negative everywhere.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly gives the conducting-cylinder surface charge density with theta measured from the applied-field direction and states the applicability condition that the electron-coated insulating cylinder must not require positive surface charge, equivalent to |E_a| <= |sigma_0|/(2 epsilon_0) under the signed convention.

Physics · test · merged mode

14. mechanics/1_27

Score 0.667

Problem Statement

Three point particles with masses m1,m2m_1, m_2m1​,m2​ and m3m_3m3​ interact with each other through the gravitational force. (a) Write down the equations of motion. (b) The system can rotate in its plane with constant and equal distances between all pairs of masses. Determine the angular frequency of the rotation when the masses are separated by a distance ddd. (c) For m1≫m3m_1 \gg m_3m1​≫m3​ and m2≫m3m_2 \gg m_3m2​≫m3​, determine the stability condition for motion of the mass m3m_3m3​ about the stationary position. Consider only motion in the orbital plane.

Extracted Answer

(a)r¨i=∑j≠iGmjrj−ri∣rj−ri∣3,i=1,2,3.(b)ω=G(m1+m2+m3)d3.(c)For m3≪m1,m2, the triangular stationary point is stable in the orbital plane ifm1m2(m1+m2)2<127,equivalently, if μ(1−μ)<127,μ=m2m1+m2.\begin{aligned} \text{(a)}\quad & \ddot{\mathbf r}_i=\sum_{j\ne i}Gm_j\frac{\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i}{|\mathbf r_j-\mathbf r_i|^3},\qquad i=1,2,3.\\[2mm] \text{(b)}\quad & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{G(m_1+m_2+m_3)}{d^3}}.\\[2mm] \text{(c)}\quad & \text{For }m_3\ll m_1,m_2,\text{ the triangular stationary point is stable in the orbital plane if}\\ & \displaystyle \frac{m_1m_2}{(m_1+m_2)^2}<\frac{1}{27},\\ & \text{equivalently, if } \mu(1-\mu)<\frac{1}{27},\quad \mu=\frac{m_2}{m_1+m_2}. \end{aligned}(a)(b)(c)​r¨i​=j=i∑​Gmj​∣rj​−ri​∣3rj​−ri​​,i=1,2,3.ω=d3G(m1​+m2​+m3​)​​.For m3​≪m1​,m2​, the triangular stationary point is stable in the orbital plane if(m1​+m2​)2m1​m2​​<271​,equivalently, if μ(1−μ)<271​,μ=m1​+m2​m2​​.​

Final Solution

Take the center of mass CCC of the system as the origin of coordinates and let the position vectors of m1,m2,m3m_1, m_2, m_3m1​,m2​,m3​ be r1,r2,r3\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2, \mathbf{r}_3r1​,r2​,r3​ respectively as shown in Fig. 1.16. Denote rij=ri−rj(i,j=1,2,3).\mathbf{r}_{ij} = \mathbf{r}_i - \mathbf{r}_j \quad (i, j = 1, 2, 3).rij​=ri​−rj​(i,j=1,2,3). (a) The motion of the iiith particle is given by mir¨i=−∑j≠i3Gmimjrij3rij,m_i \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i = - \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} \frac{G m_i m_j}{r_{ij}^3} \mathbf{r}_{ij},mi​r¨i​=−j=i∑3​rij3​Gmi​mj​​rij​, or r¨i=−∑j≠i3Gmjrij3rij(i=1,2,3).(1)\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i = - \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} \frac{G m_j}{r_{ij}^3} \mathbf{r}_{ij} \quad (i = 1, 2, 3). \tag{1}r¨i​=−j=i∑3​rij3​Gmj​​rij​(i=1,2,3).(1) Note that the minus sign is to indicate that the forces are attractive. (b) With the given condition rij=dr_{ij} = drij​=d, Eq. (1) is rewritten as r¨i=Gd3∑j≠i3mj(rj−ri)\ddot{r}_i = \frac{G}{d^3} \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} m_j (\mathbf{r}_j - \mathbf{r}_i)r¨i​=d3G​j=i∑3​mj​(rj​−ri​) =Gd3[−∑j≠i3mjri+∑j≠i3mjrj]= \frac{G}{d^3} \left[ - \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} m_j \mathbf{r}_i + \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} m_j \mathbf{r}_j \right]=d3G​​−j=i∑3​mj​ri​+j=i∑3​mj​rj​​ =Gd3[−∑j≠i3mjri−miri+∑j=13mjrj]= \frac{G}{d^3} \left[ - \sum_{j \neq i}^{3} m_j \mathbf{r}_i - m_i \mathbf{r}_i + \sum_{j = 1}^{3} m_j \mathbf{r}_j \right]=d3G​​−j=i∑3​mj​ri​−mi​ri​+j=1∑3​mj​rj​​ =Gd3[−ri∑j=13mj+∑j=13mjrj]= \frac{G}{d^3} \left[ -\mathbf{r}_i \sum_{j = 1}^{3} m_j + \sum_{j = 1}^{3} m_j \mathbf{r}_j \right]=d3G​[−ri​j=1∑3​mj​+j=1∑3​mj​rj​] =−GMd3ri,= -\frac{G M}{d^3} \mathbf{r}_i ,=−d3GM​ri​, where M=m1+m2+m3M = m_1 + m_2 + m_3M=m1​+m2​+m3​. Note that the choice of the center of mass as origin makes ∑mjrj\sum m_j \mathbf{r}_j∑mj​rj​ vanish. Thus the force on each particle points towards the center of mass of the system and is a harmonic force. With ddd constant, the system rotates about CCC with angular frequency ω=GMd3.\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G M}{d^3}} .ω=d3GM​​. (c) For m3≪m1m_3 \ll m_1m3​≪m1​ and m3≪m2m_3 \ll m_2m3​≪m2​, the equation of motion of either of the masses m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​ can be written as r¨i=−G(m1+m2)d3ri\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_i = -\frac{G (m_1 + m_2)}{d^3} \mathbf{r}_ir¨i​=−d3G(m1​+m2​)​ri​ =−G(m1+m2)r123ri,i=1,2.= -\frac{G(m_1 + m_2)}{r_{12}^3} \mathbf{r}_i , \quad i = 1, 2 .=−r123​G(m1​+m2​)​ri​,i=1,2. With the distance between m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​ constant, the system rotates about its center of mass with a constant angular frequency ω=G(m1+m2)r123.\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G(m_1 + m_2)}{r_{12}^3}} .ω=r123​G(m1​+m2​)​​. Use a rotating coordinate frame with origin at the center of mass of the system and angular frequency of rotation ω\omegaω and let the quantities r¨,r˙\ddot{\mathbf{r}}, \dot{\mathbf{r}}r¨,r˙ refer to this rotating frame. Considering the motion of particle m3m_3m3​ in the laboratory frame, we have m3(r¨3−ω2r3)=−Gm3m1r313r31−Gm3m2r323r32−2m3ω×r˙3,m_3(\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_3 - \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_3) = -\frac{Gm_3m_1}{r_{31}^3} \mathbf{r}_{31} - \frac{Gm_3m_2}{r_{32}^3} \mathbf{r}_{32} - 2m_3 \omega \times \dot{\mathbf{r}}_3,m3​(r¨3​−ω2r3​)=−r313​Gm3​m1​​r31​−r323​Gm3​m2​​r32​−2m3​ω×r˙3​, or r¨3=−Gm1r313r31−Gm2r323r32+ω2r3−2ω×r˙3.\ddot{\mathbf{r}}_3 = -\frac{Gm_1}{r_{31}^3} \mathbf{r}_{31} - \frac{Gm_2}{r_{32}^3} \mathbf{r}_{32} + \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_3 - 2\omega \times \dot{\mathbf{r}}_3.r¨3​=−r313​Gm1​​r31​−r323​Gm2​​r32​+ω2r3​−2ω×r˙3​. If m3m_3m3​ is stationary, r˙3=r¨3=0\dot{\mathbf{r}}_3 = \ddot{\mathbf{r}}_3 = 0r˙3​=r¨3​=0 and the above becomes Gm1r313(r1−r3)+Gm2r323(r2−r3)+ω2r3=0.\frac{Gm_1}{r_{31}^3} (\mathbf{r}_1 - \mathbf{r}_3) + \frac{Gm_2}{r_{32}^3} (\mathbf{r}_2 - \mathbf{r}_3) + \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_3 = 0.r313​Gm1​​(r1​−r3​)+r323​Gm2​​(r2​−r3​)+ω2r3​=0. With m1,m2≫m3m_1, m_2 \gg m_3m1​,m2​≫m3​, ∑mjrj=0\sum m_j \mathbf{r}_j = 0∑mj​rj​=0 gives m1r1≈−m2r2m_1 \mathbf{r}_1 \approx -m_2 \mathbf{r}_2m1​r1​≈−m2​r2​ and the above becomes −G(m1r313+m2r323)r3+G(m1r313−m1r323)r1+ω2r3=0.-G \left( \frac{m_1}{r_{31}^3} + \frac{m_2}{r_{32}^3} \right) \mathbf{r}_3 + G \left( \frac{m_1}{r_{31}^3} - \frac{m_1}{r_{32}^3} \right) \mathbf{r}_1 + \omega^2 \mathbf{r}_3 = 0.−G(r313​m1​​+r323​m2​​)r3​+G(r313​m1​​−r323​m1​​)r1​+ω2r3​=0. This relation shows that r3\mathbf{r}_3r3​ is parallel to r1\mathbf{r}_1r1​ and thus the stationary position of m3m_3m3​ lies on the line joining m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​.
