At this position, the attractions of m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​ are balanced. Consider now a small displacement being applied to m3m_3m3​ at this stationary position. If the displacement is along the line joining m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​, say toward m1m_1m1​, the attraction by m1m_1m1​ is enhanced and that by m2m_2m2​ is reduced. Then m3m_3m3​ will continue to move toward m1m_1m1​ and the equilibrium is unstable. On the other hand, if the displacement is normal to the line joining m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​, both the attractions by m1m_1m1​ and m2m_2m2​ will have a component toward the stationary position and will restore m3m_3m3​ to this position. Thus the equilibrium is stable. Therefore the equilibrium is stable against a transverse perturbation but unstable against a longitudinal one.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly gives the three-body Newtonian equations of motion in equivalent vector form.
  • (b) Correctly gives the angular frequency for equal pairwise separation d as sqrt(G(m1+m2+m3)/d^3).
  • (c) The reference solution describes the collinear stationary position for m3 and states it is stable only for transverse perturbations and unstable for longitudinal perturbations. The candidate instead gives the triangular Lagrange-point stability criterion, which is not the referenced answer.

Physics · test · merged mode

15. statistics/2-68

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Consider a photon gas enclosed in a volume VVV and in equilibrium at temperature TTT. The photon is a massless particle, so that ϵ=pc\epsilon = pcϵ=pc. (a) What is the chemical potential of the gas? (b) Determine how the number of photons in the volume depends upon the temperature. (c) One may write the energy density in the form EV=∫0∞ρ(ω)dω.\frac{E}{V} = \int_0^\infty \rho (\omega) d\omega .VE​=∫0∞​ρ(ω)dω. Determine the form of ρ(ω)\rho(\omega)ρ(ω), the spectral density of the energy. (d) What is the temperature dependence of the energy E‾\overline{E}E?

Extracted Answer

(a) μ=0;(b) N=2ζ(3)π2V(kBTℏc)3∝VT3;(c) ρ(ω)=ℏω3π2c3(eℏω/kBT−1);(d) E‾=π2kB415ℏ3c3VT4∝VT4.\text{(a) }\mu=0;\quad \text{(b) }N=\frac{2\zeta(3)}{\pi^2}V\left(\frac{k_B T}{\hbar c}\right)^3\propto VT^3;\quad \text{(c) }\rho(\omega)=\frac{\hbar \omega^3}{\pi^2 c^3\left(e^{\hbar\omega/k_BT}-1\right)};\quad \text{(d) }\overline E=\frac{\pi^2 k_B^4}{15\hbar^3c^3}VT^4\propto VT^4.(a) μ=0;(b) N=π22ζ(3)​V(ℏckB​T​)3∝VT3;(c) ρ(ω)=π2c3(eℏω/kB​T−1)ℏω3​;(d) E=15ℏ3c3π2kB4​​VT4∝VT4.

Final Solution

(a) The chemical potential of the photon gas is zero. Since the number of photons is not conserved at a given temperature and volume, the average photon number is determined by the expression (∂F∂N‾)T,V=0\left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial \overline N} \right)_{T, V} = 0(∂N∂F​)T,V​=0, then μ=(∂F∂N‾)T,V=0.\mu = \left( \frac{\partial F}{\partial \overline N} \right)_{T, V} = 0.μ=(∂N∂F​)T,V​=0. (b) The density of states is 8πVp2dp/h38 \pi V p^2 dp / h^38πVp2dp/h3, or Vω2dω/πc3V \omega^2 d\omega / \pi c^3Vω2dω/πc3. Then the number of photons is N‾=∫Vπ2c3ω21eℏω/kT−1 dω=Vπ2c3(kTℏ)3∫0∞α2dαeα−1∝T3.\overline N = \int \frac{V}{\pi^2 c^3} \omega^2 \frac{1}{e^{\hbar \omega / kT} - 1} \, d\omega = \frac{V}{\pi^2 c^3} \left( \frac{kT}{\hbar} \right)^3 \int_0^\infty \frac{\alpha^2 d\alpha}{e^\alpha - 1} \propto T^3.N=∫π2c3V​ω2eℏω/kT−11​dω=π2c3V​(ℏkT​)3∫0∞​eα−1α2dα​∝T3. (c), (d) E‾V=∫ω2π2c3ℏωeℏω/kT−1 dω=(kT)4π2c3ℏ3∫ξ3 dξeξ−1.\frac{\overline{E}}{V} = \int \frac{\omega^2}{\pi^2 c^3} \frac{\hbar \omega}{e^{\hbar \omega / kT} - 1} \, d\omega = \frac{(kT)^4}{\pi^2 c^3 \hbar^3} \int \frac{\xi^3 \, d \xi}{e^\xi - 1}.VE​=∫π2c3ω2​eℏω/kT−1ℏω​dω=π2c3ℏ3(kT)4​∫eξ−1ξ3dξ​. Hence ρ(ω)=ℏπ2c3ω3eℏω/kT−1,\rho(\omega) = \frac{\hbar}{\pi^2 c^3} \frac{\omega^3}{e^{\hbar \omega / kT} - 1},ρ(ω)=π2c3ℏ​eℏω/kT−1ω3​, and E‾∝T4\overline{E} \propto T^4E∝T4.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correct: the photon gas chemical potential is zero.
  • (b) Correct: gives the photon number proportional to V T^3 with the standard coefficient equivalent to the reference integral.
  • (c) Correct: the spectral energy density matches the Planck form in angular frequency used by the reference solution.
  • (d) Correct: gives total energy proportional to V T^4 with the standard blackbody coefficient, consistent with the reference.

Physics · test · merged mode

16. atomic/4-24

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

State whether the following processes are possible or impossible: (a) A single photon strikes a stationary electron and gives up all its energy to the electron. (b) A single photon in empty space is transformed into an electron and a positron. (c) A fast positron and a stationary electron annihilate, producing only one photon.

Extracted Answer

(a) Impossible; (b) Impossible; (c) Impossible.\text{(a) Impossible; (b) Impossible; (c) Impossible.}(a) Impossible; (b) Impossible; (c) Impossible.

Final Solution

All the three reactions cannot take place because in each case energy and momentum cannot be both conserved. (a) For the process γ+e→e′,\gamma + e \rightarrow e',γ+e→e′, conservation of the invariant mass squared, S=(Eγ+me)2−pγ2=2meEγ+me2=Ee′2−pe′2=me2,S = (E_\gamma + m_e)^2 - p_\gamma^2 = 2m_eE_\gamma + m_e^2 = E_{e'}^2 - p_{e'}^2 = m_e^2,S=(Eγ​+me​)2−pγ2​=2me​Eγ​+me2​=Ee′2​−pe′2​=me2​, leads to meEγ=0m_eE_\gamma = 0me​Eγ​=0, which contradicts the fact that neither EγE_\gammaEγ​ nor mem_eme​ is zero. (b) In the process γ→e++e−\gamma \rightarrow e^+ + e^-γ→e++e−, let the energies and momenta of the produced e+e^+e+ and e−e^-e− be E1,E2,p1,p2E_1, E_2, \mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2E1​,E2​,p1​,p2​ respectively. The invariant mass squared of the initial state is S(γ)=Eγ2−pγ2=0,S(\gamma) = E_\gamma^2 - p_\gamma^2 = 0,S(γ)=Eγ2​−pγ2​=0, while for the final state it is S(e+e−)=(E1+E2)2−(p1+p2)2=2me2+2(E1E2−p1p2cos⁡θ)≥2me2,S(e^+ e^-) = (E_1 + E_2)^2 - (\mathbf{p}_1 + \mathbf{p}_2)^2 = 2m_e^2 + 2(E_1E_2 - p_1p_2 \cos \theta) \geq 2m_e^2,S(e+e−)=(E1​+E2​)2−(p1​+p2​)2=2me2​+2(E1​E2​−p1​p2​cosθ)≥2me2​, where θ\thetaθ is the angle between p1\mathbf{p}_1p1​ and p2\mathbf{p}_2p2​. As S(γ)≠S(e+e−)S(\gamma) \neq S(e^+ e^-)S(γ)=S(e+e−), its invariance is violated and the reaction cannot take place. (c) The reaction is the inverse of that in (b). It similarly cannot take place.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly states the process is impossible, matching the reference result that energy and momentum cannot both be conserved.
  • (b) Correctly states that a single photon in empty space cannot transform into an electron-positron pair.
  • (c) Correctly states that annihilation into only one photon is impossible, matching the reference.

Physics · test · merged mode

17. optics/1-23

Score 0.000

Problem Statement

As shown in Fig. 1.29, the image which would be cast by the converging lens alone has a distance of 0.5 cm between top and bottom. Calculate, of the final image: (a) the position. (b) size.

Extracted Answer

(a) Underdetermined without Fig. 1.29; generally v=fuu−f from the second lens.  (b) Underdetermined; generally hf=0.5∣fu−f∣ cm.\text{(a) Underdetermined without Fig. 1.29; generally }v=\frac{fu}{u-f}\text{ from the second lens. }\text{ (b) Underdetermined; generally }h_f=0.5\left|\frac{f}{u-f}\right|\text{ cm.}(a) Underdetermined without Fig. 1.29; generally v=u−ffu​ from the second lens.  (b) Underdetermined; generally hf​=0.5​u−ff​​ cm.

Final Solution

﻿An object infinite distance away finds its image on the back focal plane of the converging lens, i.e., at 20−15=520 - 15 = 520−15=5 cm\text{cm}cm behind the diverging lens. The Gaussian lens formula 1u+1v=1f\frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{f}u1​+v1​=f1​ applied to the diverging lens then gives for u=−5 cmu = -5 \, \text{cm}u=−5cm and f=−10f = -10f=−10 cm\text{cm}cm, v=10v = 10v=10 cm\text{cm}cm. The lateral magnification is m=hh′=105=2.m = \frac{h}{h'} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.m=h′h​=510​=2. Hence the size of the final image is 0.5×2=1 cm.0.5 \times 2 = 1 \, \text{cm}.0.5×2=1cm. Fig. 1.30 gives the ray diagram:

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Reference gives a definite final image position of v = 10 cm for the diverging lens; candidate instead says underdetermined and gives only a general formula.
  • (b) Reference gives final image size 1 cm; candidate instead says underdetermined and gives only a general expression.

Physics · test · merged mode

18. quantum/2-2001

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

An electron is confined in a three-dimensional infinite potential well. The sides parallel to the *x-*, *y-*, and *z*-axes are of length LLL each. (a) Write the appropriate Schrödinger equation. (b) Write the time-independent wave function corresponding to the state of the lowest possible energy. (c) Give an expression for the number of states, NNN, having energy less than some given EEE. Assume N≫1N \gg 1N≫1.

Extracted Answer

(a)−ℏ22me(∂2ψ∂x2+∂2ψ∂y2+∂2ψ∂z2)=Eψ,0<x,y,z<L,ψ=0 on x,y,z=0,L.(b)ψ111(x,y,z)=(2L)3/2sin⁡πxLsin⁡πyLsin⁡πzL,E111=3π2ℏ22meL2.(c)Enxnynz=π2ℏ22meL2(nx2+ny2+nz2),N(E)≃184π3(2meL2Eπ2ℏ2)3/2=L36π2(2meEℏ2)3/2. Including electron spin degeneracy, multiply by 2.\begin{aligned} \text{(a)}\quad& -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\left(\frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial x^2} +\frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial y^2} +\frac{\partial^2\psi}{\partial z^2}\right)=E\psi, \quad 0<x,y,z<L,\\ &\psi=0\ \text{on}\ x,y,z=0,L.\\[4pt] \text{(b)}\quad& \psi_{111}(x,y,z)=\left(\frac{2}{L}\right)^{3/2} \sin\frac{\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{\pi y}{L}\sin\frac{\pi z}{L},\\ &E_{111}=\frac{3\pi^2\hbar^2}{2m_eL^2}.\\[4pt] \text{(c)}\quad& E_{n_xn_yn_z}=\frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2m_eL^2} \left(n_x^2+n_y^2+n_z^2\right),\\ &N(E)\simeq \frac{1}{8}\frac{4\pi}{3} \left(\frac{2m_eL^2E}{\pi^2\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} =\frac{L^3}{6\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_eE}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}. \ \text{Including electron spin degeneracy, multiply by }2. \end{aligned}(a)(b)(c)​−2me​ℏ2​(∂x2∂2ψ​+∂y2∂2ψ​+∂z2∂2ψ​)=Eψ,0<x,y,z<L,ψ=0 on x,y,z=0,L.ψ111​(x,y,z)=(L2​)3/2sinLπx​sinLπy​sinLπz​,E111​=2me​L23π2ℏ2​.Enx​ny​nz​​=2me​L2π2ℏ2​(nx2​+ny2​+nz2​),N(E)≃81​34π​(π2ℏ22me​L2E​)3/2=6π2L3​(ℏ22me​E​)3/2. Including electron spin degeneracy, multiply by 2.​

Final Solution

(a) The Schrödinger equation is iℏ∂ψ(r,t)/∂t=−(ℏ2/2m)∇2ψ(r,t),0≤x,y,z≤L,i \hbar \partial \psi (r, t)/ \partial t = - \left( \hbar^2 / 2m \right) \nabla^2 \psi (r, t), \quad 0 \leq x, y, z \leq L,iℏ∂ψ(r,t)/∂t=−(ℏ2/2m)∇2ψ(r,t),0≤x,y,z≤L, ψ=0,otherwise.\psi = 0, \quad \text{otherwise.}ψ=0,otherwise. (b) By separation of variables, we can take that the wave function to be the product of three wave functions each of a one-dimensional infinite well potential. The wave function of the lowest energy level is ψ111(x,y,z)=ψ1(x)ψ1(y)ψ1(z),\psi_{111}(x, y, z) = \psi_1(x) \psi_1(y) \psi_1(z),ψ111​(x,y,z)=ψ1​(x)ψ1​(y)ψ1​(z), where ψ1(x)=2Lsin⁡(πLx),etc.\psi_1(x) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}} \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{L} x \right), \quad \text{etc.}ψ1​(x)=L2​​sin(Lπ​x),etc. Thus ψ111(x,y,z)=(2L)3/2sin⁡(πxL)sin⁡(πyL)sin⁡(πzL).\psi_{111}(x, y, z) = \left( \frac{2}{L} \right)^{3/2} \sin \left( \frac{\pi x}{L} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi y}{L} \right) \sin \left( \frac{\pi z}{L} \right).ψ111​(x,y,z)=(L2​)3/2sin(Lπx​)sin(Lπy​)sin(Lπz​). The corresponding energy is E111=3ℏ2π2/2mL2E_{111} = 3 \hbar^2 \pi^2 / 2mL^2E111​=3ℏ2π2/2mL2. (c) For a set of quantum numbers nx,ny,nzn_x, n_y, n_znx​,ny​,nz​ for the three dimensions, the energy is E=ℏ2π22mL2(nx2+ny2+nz2).E = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2mL^2} \left( n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2 \right).E=2mL2ℏ2π2​(nx2​+ny2​+nz2​). Hence the number NNN of states whose energy is less than or equal to EEE is equal to the number of sets of three positive integers nx,ny,nzn_x, n_y, n_znx​,ny​,nz​ satisfying the inequality nx2+ny2+nz2≤2mL2Eℏ2π2.n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2 \leq \frac{2mL^2E}{\hbar^2 \pi^2}.nx2​+ny2​+nz2​≤ℏ2π22mL2E​. Consider a Cartesian coordinate system of axes nx,ny,nn_x, n_y, nnx​,ny​,n. The number NNN required is numerically equal to the volume in the first quadrant of a sphere of radius (2mL2E/ℏ2π2)1/2(2mL^2E/\hbar^2 \pi^2)^{1/2}(2mL2E/ℏ2π2)1/2, provided N≥1N \geq 1N≥1. Thus N=18⋅4π3⋅(2mL2ℏ2π2E)3/2=4π3(mL22ℏ2π2E)3/2.N = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{4 \pi}{3} \cdot \left( \frac{2mL^2}{\hbar^2 \pi^2} E \right)^{3/2} = \frac{4 \pi}{3} \left( \frac{mL^2}{2 \hbar^2 \pi^2 E} \right)^{3/2}.N=81​⋅34π​⋅(ℏ2π22mL2​E)3/2=34π​(2ℏ2π2EmL2​)3/2.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) The candidate gives the correct time-independent Schrödinger equation for a free electron inside the 3D infinite well with appropriate boundary conditions; this is an acceptable equivalent stationary-state form of the reference equation.
  • (b) The candidate gives the correctly normalized ground-state wave function with quantum numbers (1,1,1), and the stated energy is also correct.
  • (c) The candidate gives the correct energy spectrum and the large-N first-octant sphere counting formula, equivalently simplified to the standard density-of-states expression; noting optional spin degeneracy does not materially change the requested spatial-state count.

Physics · test · merged mode

19. Quantum Mechanics/27-5

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Positronium is an atom made up of an electron and a positron (electron anti-particle). (a) As a "zeroth" order approximation, neglect everything (e.g., fine structure, hyperfine structure, etc.) except the Coulombic interaction between the electron and positron. Relate the "zeroth" order positronium energies to those of hydrogen. (b) Taking spin into account, each of the levels of part (a) are 4-degenerate (since the electron and positron are both spin-1/2 particles). Taking spin-spin interactions into account, H=H0−Aμp⋅μeH = H_0 - A\mu_p \cdot \mu_eH=H0​−Aμp​⋅μe​, where μ\muμ is the magnetic moment, ppp stands for positron and eee for electron. Place the positronium in a uniform and constant external magnetic field, B\mathbf{B}B. What are the resulting energies of a level?

Extracted Answer

(a) En,Ps(0)=μPsμHEn,H(0),μPs=me2,μH=meMpme+Mp.Thus En,Ps(0)≃12En,H(0)=−6.80 eVn2.(b) For a fixed zeroth-order level E0=En,Ps(0), with μ=gμB/2,E1,+1=E1,−1=E0+Aμ2,E±=E0−Aμ2±(2Aμ2)2+(2μB)2.\begin{gathered} \text{(a) }E_{n,\rm Ps}^{(0)}=\frac{\mu_{\rm Ps}}{\mu_{\rm H}}E_{n,\rm H}^{(0)},\qquad \mu_{\rm Ps}=\frac{m_e}{2},\quad \mu_{\rm H}=\frac{m_e M_p}{m_e+M_p}.\\ \text{Thus }E_{n,\rm Ps}^{(0)}\simeq \frac12E_{n,\rm H}^{(0)} =-\frac{6.80\ \mathrm{eV}}{n^2}.\\[4pt] \text{(b) For a fixed zeroth-order level }E_0=E_{n,\rm Ps}^{(0)},\text{ with }\mu=g\mu_B/2,\\ E_{1,+1}=E_{1,-1}=E_0+A\mu^2,\\ E_{\pm}=E_0-A\mu^2\pm\sqrt{(2A\mu^2)^2+(2\mu B)^2}. \end{gathered}(a) En,Ps(0)​=μH​μPs​​En,H(0)​,μPs​=2me​​,μH​=me​+Mp​me​Mp​​.Thus En,Ps(0)​≃21​En,H(0)​=−n26.80 eV​.(b) For a fixed zeroth-order level E0​=En,Ps(0)​, with μ=gμB​/2,E1,+1​=E1,−1​=E0​+Aμ2,E±​=E0​−Aμ2±(2Aμ2)2+(2μB)2​.​

Final Solution

(a) The Bohr Hydrogen energies are En=−12n2α2mec2E_n = - \frac{1}{2n^2} \alpha^2 m_ec^2En​=−2n21​α2me​c2, where α=e2/4πϵ0ℏc≈1/137\alpha = e^2/4\pi \epsilon_0 \hbar c \approx 1/137α=e2/4πϵ0​ℏc≈1/137 is the fine structure constant. To correct for finite proton mass, one uses the reduced electron mass μ=mempme+mp\mu = \frac{m_e m_p}{m_e + m_p}μ=me​+mp​me​mp​​. In this case, mp=mem_p = m_emp​=me​, so μ=me/2\mu = m_e/2μ=me​/2. Thus, *Positronium energies are half of Bohr energies*. (b) μ=γS\mu = \gamma Sμ=γS, where γ\gammaγ is gyromagnetic ratio. γ=−gμB/ℏ\gamma = -g\mu_B/\hbarγ=−gμB​/ℏ, where ggg is the g-factor. We have ge=−gp=2g_e = -g_p = 2ge​=−gp​=2 and so μe=−2μBℏSe\mu_e = -\frac{2\mu_B}{\hbar} S_eμe​=−ℏ2μB​​Se​, and μp=2μBℏSp\mu_p = \frac{2\mu_B}{\hbar} S_pμp​=ℏ2μB​​Sp​. The Hamiltonian is H=H0−Aμp⋅μe−(μe+μp)⋅B=H0+(2μBℏ)2ASp⋅Se+(2μBℏ)B(Sez−Spz)\begin{aligned} H & =H_0-A \boldsymbol{\mu}_p \cdot \boldsymbol{\mu}_e-\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}_e+\boldsymbol{\mu}_p\right) \cdot \mathbf{B} \\ & =H_0+\left(\frac{2 \mu_B}{\hbar}\right)^2 A \mathbf{S}_p \cdot \mathbf{S}_e+\left(\frac{2 \mu_B}{\hbar}\right) B\left(S_e^z-S_p^z\right) \end{aligned}H​=H0​−Aμp​⋅μe​−(μe​+μp​)⋅B=H0​+(ℏ2μB​​)2ASp​⋅Se​+(ℏ2μB​​)B(Sez​−Spz​)​ (27.5.1) Let ∣1⟩=∣↑↑⟩|1\rangle = |\uparrow \uparrow\rangle∣1⟩=∣↑↑⟩, ∣2⟩=∣↓↓⟩|2\rangle = |\downarrow \downarrow\rangle∣2⟩=∣↓↓⟩, ∣3⟩=12(∣↑↓⟩+∣↓↑⟩)|3\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (|\uparrow \downarrow\rangle + |\downarrow \uparrow\rangle)∣3⟩=2​1​(∣↑↓⟩+∣↓↑⟩) and ∣4⟩=12(∣↑↓⟩−∣↓↑⟩)|4\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} (|\uparrow \downarrow\rangle - |\downarrow \uparrow\rangle)∣4⟩=2​1​(∣↑↓⟩−∣↓↑⟩), where the first spin is the electron and the second the positron. In the ∣SmS⟩\left|S m_S\right\rangle∣SmS​⟩ basis, ∣1⟩=∣11⟩|1\rangle = |11\rangle∣1⟩=∣11⟩, ∣2⟩=∣1,−1⟩|2\rangle = |1, -1\rangle∣2⟩=∣1,−1⟩, ∣3⟩=∣10⟩|3\rangle = |10\rangle∣3⟩=∣10⟩, ∣4⟩=∣00⟩|4\rangle = |00\rangle∣4⟩=∣00⟩. Sp⋅Se=12(S2−Se2−Sp2)=ℏ24(2S2ℏ2−3)\mathbf{S}_p \cdot \mathbf{S}_e=\frac{1}{2}\left(S^2-S_e^2-S_p^2\right)=\frac{\hbar^2}{4}\left(\frac{2 S^2}{\hbar^2}-3\right)Sp​⋅Se​=21​(S2−Se2​−Sp2​)=4ℏ2​(ℏ22S2​−3) which is ℏ24\frac{\hbar^2}{4}4ℏ2​ for S=1S = 1S=1 and −34ℏ2-\frac{3}{4} \hbar^2−43​ℏ2 for S=0S = 0S=0. Thus, (2μBℏ)2Sp⋅Se\left( \frac{2\mu_B}{\hbar} \right)^2 S_p \cdot S_e(ℏ2μB​​)2Sp​⋅Se​ has the value μB2\mu_B^2μB2​ on S=1S = 1S=1 states and −3μB2-3 \mu_B^2−3μB2​ on S=0S = 0S=0 states. Write the magnetic field term as BμB(σez−σpz)B \mu_B\left(\sigma_e^z-\sigma_p^z\right)BμB​(σez​−σpz​). It is clear that this operator vanishes on ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩ and ∣2⟩|2\rangle∣2⟩. However, σez∣3⟩=∣4⟩\sigma_e^z |3\rangle = |4\rangleσez​∣3⟩=∣4⟩ and σpz∣3⟩=−∣4⟩\sigma_p^z |3\rangle = -|4\rangleσpz​∣3⟩=−∣4⟩, etc. Hence, in this basis, the Hamiltonian reads H=(H0+AμB20000H0+AμB20000H0+AμB22BμB002BμBH0−3AμB2)H = \begin{pmatrix} H_0 + A\mu_B^2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & H_0 + A\mu_B^2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & H_0 + A\mu_B^2 & 2B\mu_B \\ 0 & 0 & 2B\mu_B & H_0 - 3A\mu_B^2 \end{pmatrix}H=​H0​+AμB2​000​0H0​+AμB2​00​00H0​+AμB2​2BμB​​002BμB​H0​−3AμB2​​​ (27.5.2) Forget H0H_0H0​ since that does not affect diagonalization. We must diagonalize (12β2β−3)\left( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2\beta \\ 2\beta & -3 \end{array} \right)(12β​2β−3​), where β=B/AμB\beta = B/A\mu_Bβ=B/AμB​. The secular equation reads (λ−1)(λ+3)−4β2=λ2+2λ−(3+4β2)=0(\lambda - 1)(\lambda + 3) - 4 \beta^2 = \lambda^2 + 2\lambda - (3 + 4 \beta^2) = 0(λ−1)(λ+3)−4β2=λ2+2λ−(3+4β2)=0 with solutions λ±=−1±1+3+4β2=−1±21+β2\lambda_{\pm} = -1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 3 + 4 \beta^2 = -1 \pm 2\sqrt{1 + \beta^2}}λ±​=−1±1+3+4β2=−1±21+β2​​. Thus, 1. ∣1⟩|1\rangle∣1⟩ and ∣2⟩|2\rangle∣2⟩ remain unchanged with energy H0+AμB2H_0 + A\mu_B^2H0​+AμB2​, 2. ∣3⟩|3\rangle∣3⟩ transforms with energy H0+AμB2+2AμB2[1+β2−1],H_0 + A\mu_B^2 + 2A\mu_B^2 \left[ \sqrt{1+\beta^2} - 1 \right],H0​+AμB2​+2AμB2​[1+β2​−1], 3. ∣4⟩|4\rangle∣4⟩ transforms with energy H0−3AμB2−2AμB2[1+β2−1].H_0 - 3A\mu_B^2 - 2A\mu_B^2 \left[ \sqrt{1+\beta^2} - 1 \right].H0​−3AμB2​−2AμB2​[1+β2​−1]. (27.5.3)

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Correctly states that positronium energies scale with the reduced mass and are approximately half the hydrogen Bohr energies, giving the correct -6.80 eV/n^2 form.
  • (b) Gives the two unchanged triplet energies and the two mixed m=0 energies in a form equivalent to diagonalizing the reference 2x2 Hamiltonian, with mu = mu_B for g = 2.

Physics · test · merged mode

20. electro/1_45

Score 1.000

Problem Statement

Two conductors are embedded in a material of conductivity 10−4Ω/m10^{-4} \Omega/m10−4Ω/m and dielectric constant ε=80ε0\varepsilon = 80 \varepsilon_0ε=80ε0​. The resistance between the two conductors is measured to be 105Ω10^5 \Omega105Ω. (a) Derive an equation for the capacitance between the two conductors. (b) calculate its value.

Extracted Answer

(a) C=εσR(b) C=7.08×10−11 F≈70.8 pF\text{(a) } C=\frac{\varepsilon}{\sigma R}\quad \text{(b) } C=7.08\times 10^{-11}\ \mathrm{F}\approx 70.8\ \mathrm{pF}(a) C=σRε​(b) C=7.08×10−11 F≈70.8 pF

Final Solution

### Solution: Suppose that the two conductors carry free charges QQQ and −Q-Q−Q. Consider a closed surface enclosing the conductor with the charge QQQ (but not the other conductor). We have, using Ohm’s and Gauss’ laws, I=∮j⋅dS=∮σE⋅dS=σ∮E⋅dS=σQε.I = \oint \mathbf{j} \cdot dS = \oint \sigma \mathbf{E} \cdot dS = \sigma \oint \mathbf{E} \cdot dS = \sigma \frac{Q}{\varepsilon}.I=∮j⋅dS=∮σE⋅dS=σ∮E⋅dS=σεQ​. If the potential difference between the two conductors is VVV, we have V=IR=σQεRV = IR = \frac{\sigma Q}{\varepsilon} RV=IR=εσQ​R, whence C=QV=εσR.C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\sigma R}.C=VQ​=σRε​. Numerically the capacitance between the conductors is C=80×8.85×10−1210−4×105=7.08×10−11 F.C = \frac{80 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-4} \times 10^5} = 7.08 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{F}.C=10−4×10580×8.85×10−12​=7.08×10−11F.

Judge Rationale

  • (a) Gives the correct relation C = ε/(σR) for the capacitance between the conductors.
  • (b) Computes the capacitance as 7.08×10^-11 F, equivalent to the reference value; 70.8 pF is a correct conversion.
